topics.title: Introduction to Energy Conversion - Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Energy Conversion - Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Energy? (KTU 2024 Formal Definition)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OECHT614**, energy is formally defined as:

> **Energy** is the fundamental scalar physical quantity that represents the capacity of a system to perform **work** or to transfer **heat**. It is a conserved quantity — neither created nor destroyed — but can be transformed from one form to another, as governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**.

The SI unit of energy is the **Joule (J)**, where **1 J = 1 N·m**. Other commonly used units are **kWh, kcal, BTU, and eV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"Energy is the ability to do work. It is a property of matter and a conserved quantity that manifests in many interchangeable forms, including mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, nuclear, and radiant."*

## 1.2 What is Energy Conversion?

**Energy Conversion** (or energy transformation) is the process of changing energy from one form to another. In engineering systems, energy conversion is the heart of every prime mover, generator, and consumer device.

For example, in a **thermal power plant**:
$$\text{Chemical Energy (Coal)} \longrightarrow \text{Thermal Energy (Steam)} \longrightarrow \text{Mechanical Energy (Turbine)} \longrightarrow \text{Electrical Energy (Generator)}$$

## 1.3 What are Energy Resources?

**Energy Resources** are the naturally occurring or cultivated sources from which useful energy can be harvested. KTU categorizes them into:
- **Conventional (Non-Renewable)** — Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium)
- **Non-Conventional (Renewable)** — Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal, Hydrogen

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Account of the Universe" 🏦

Imagine a **universal energy bank account** that has a fixed balance equal to the total energy of the universe (this never changes). You cannot deposit or withdraw from this total balance, but you can **convert** one currency into another.

| Energy Currency | Real-world Form |
|---|---|
| 💵 Dollar | Mechanical (KE / PE) |
| 💶 Euro | Electrical |
| 💷 Pound | Thermal / Heat |
| ₹ Rupee | Chemical / Fuel |
| ¥ Yen | Nuclear / Radiant |

Just as you pay a small **transaction fee (efficiency loss)** when exchanging currency, every energy conversion loses some energy as **waste heat** (governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**). You can never get a 100% favorable exchange rate!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Energy is neither created nor destroyed, but its *quality* (or *exergy*) always decreases during conversion. This is the deep truth behind the Second Law.

## 1.5 Fundamental Physical Constants (KTU Board Favourite)

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s** |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **$5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/mol·K** |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Solar constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Primary Forms of Energy

Every energy problem in KTU reduces to one or a combination of the following **eight primary forms**:

1. **Mechanical Energy** — sum of kinetic and potential energy of bulk matter.
2. **Thermal Energy** — internal kinetic energy of molecules (sensible + latent).
3. **Chemical Energy** — energy stored in molecular bonds (fossil fuels, biomass, batteries).
4. **Electrical Energy** — energy of moving electrons (current × voltage × time).
5. **Nuclear Energy** — energy from strong-force binding in the nucleus (fission / fusion).
6. **Radiant (Electromagnetic) Energy** — energy carried by photons (solar, light, infrared).
7. **Chemical Potential / Electrochemical Energy** — redox reactions, fuel cells.
8. **Mass Energy** — the rest-mass equivalent via $E = mc^2$.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Energy Engineering

### Pillar 1: First Law of Thermodynamics (Conservation)
The total energy of an isolated system is constant. Mathematically:
$$\Delta E_{system} = Q - W$$
where $Q$ = heat supplied to the system and $W$ = work done **by** the system.

### Pillar 2: Second Law of Thermodynamics (Direction & Quality)
Energy spontaneously flows from hot to cold, and the entropy of the universe always increases. No heat engine can be **100% efficient** (Kelvin-Planck statement). The Carnot efficiency is the upper limit:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$
where $T_C$ and $T_H$ are in **Kelvin**.

### Pillar 3: Energy Conversion Chain
Every practical system has a chain:
$$\text{Source} \longrightarrow \text{Converter} \longrightarrow \text{End Use} \longrightarrow \text{Waste}$$

## 2.3 Classification of Energy Resources (KTU Mandatory)

### A. By Depletion Rate

| Category | Definition | Examples | Replenish Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Renewable** | Naturally replenished, sustainable | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Biomass, Geothermal | Continuous / Decades |
| **Non-Renewable** | Finite, deplete with use | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium | Millions of years |

### B. By Commercial Use (KTU Board Standard)

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Primary (Commercial)** | Coal, Oil, Gas, Hydro, Nuclear, Solar, Wind |
| **Secondary (Derived)** | Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Hydrogen, Coke |

### C. By Availability (Indian Context)
- **Conventional (Traditional)** — Firewood, Cow-dung, Agricultural waste
- **Non-Conventional (Modern)** — Solar PV, Wind turbines, OTEC, MHD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** "Renewable ≠ Non-Conventional" and "Non-Renewable ≠ Conventional". **Hydro is both renewable AND conventional.** Nuclear is non-renewable but non-conventional. Memorize this distinction.

## 2.4 Global & Indian Energy Scenario (Static GK for KTU)

- World primary energy mix (2023): Oil **~31%**, Coal **~27%**, Gas **~24%**, Renewables **~13%**, Nuclear **~4%**, Hydro **~1%**.
- India imports **>85%** of its crude oil requirements.
- India is the **3rd largest** energy consumer globally (after China & USA).
- India's installed renewable capacity (2024): **~190 GW** (target 500 GW by 2030).
- Tidal energy potential at Gulf of Kutch & Gulf of Cambay.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | $m$ = mass, $v$ = velocity |
| 2 | Potential Energy | $PE = m g h$ | J | $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 3 | Mass-Energy | $E = m c^2$ | J | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 4 | Electrical Energy | $E = V I t$ | J | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $t$ in s |
| 5 | Heat (Sensible) | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | J | $c$ = specific heat (J/kg·K) |
| 6 | Heat (Latent) | $Q = m L$ | J | $L$ = latent heat (J/kg) |
| 7 | Power | $P = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{dE}{dt}$ | W (J/s) | Time-rate of energy |
| 8 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Always $\eta < 1$ |
| 9 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | Upper limit |
| 10 | Wind Power | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | W | $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³) |
| 11 | Solar Insolation | $E = G_{sc} \times A \times t$ | J | $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m² |
| 12 | Electrical Power | $P = V I \cos\phi$ | W | AC circuits |
| 13 | Hydroelectric Power | $P = \eta \rho g Q h$ | W | $Q$ = discharge (m³/s) |
| 14 | Energy Content of Fuel | $E = m \times CV$ | J | $CV$ = calorific value (J/kg) |
| 15 | Power of Falling Mass | $P = \rho g Q h \eta$ | W | Gravitational PE → Power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In all energy conversion problems, the **efficiency** $\eta$ is the ratio of **useful output** to **total input**, and it is ALWAYS less than 1 (or 100%).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Power Plants:** Convert chemical/nuclear/radiant energy into electrical energy via a thermodynamic cycle.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Convert chemical (petrol) → thermal → mechanical.
- **Photovoltaic Cells:** Direct conversion of radiant → electrical (no thermal stage).
- **Fuel Cells:** Direct chemical → electrical (bypassing the Carnot limit).
- **Wind Turbines:** Kinetic energy of air → mechanical → electrical.
- **Hydel Plants:** Potential energy of stored water → mechanical → electrical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinetic Energy from Work-Energy Theorem

The kinetic energy of a body is the work required to accelerate it from rest to a velocity $v$.

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's Second Law.
$$F = m a = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by displacement $dx$.
$$F \, dx = m v \, dv$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from initial state (0, $v_1$) to final state ($x$, $v_2$).
$$\int_0^x F \, dx = m \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v \, dv$$

**Step 4:** The left side is the work done $W$. The right side integrates as:
$$W = m \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{v_1}^{v_2} = \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$$

**Step 5:** Since the body starts from rest, $v_1 = 0$, and we get the final expression for kinetic energy.
$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

> **Logical Note:** This derivation uses the **work-energy theorem**, which states that the net work done on a body equals the change in its kinetic energy.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gravitational Potential Energy

The potential energy is the work done against gravity to raise a body to height $h$.

**Step 1:** The force required to lift a body of mass $m$ at constant velocity is:
$$F = m g$$

**Step 2:** Work done in raising through a height $h$ at constant velocity:
$$W = F \times h = m g h$$

**Step 3:** This work is stored as gravitational potential energy.
$$PE = m g h \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydroelectric Power (KTU Favourite)

Consider a volume $V$ of water at height $h$ above the turbine. Mass of water = $\rho V$, where $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ is the density of water.

**Step 1:** Potential energy of the water mass:
$$PE = m g h = \rho V g h$$

**Step 2:** If water flows at a discharge rate $Q$ (volume per second, in m³/s), the energy per second (i.e., power) is:
$$P_{input} = \rho g h \frac{V}{t} = \rho g h Q$$

**Step 3:** Apply turbine-generator efficiency $\eta$ to get the electrical output power.
$$P_{output} = \eta \, \rho \, g \, h \, Q \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting numerical values for water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):
$$P = 9810 \, \eta \, h \, Q \quad \text{(kW, if h in m, Q in m³/s)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Wind Power (Betz Limit Context)

The power available in a wind stream of cross-section $A$, velocity $v$, and air density $\rho$ is the kinetic energy flux.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of air through the rotor:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy per unit mass of air:
$$e = \frac{1}{2} v^2$$

**Step 3:** Wind power (rate of kinetic energy flow):
$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times e = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

**Step 4:** The **Betz limit** states that no turbine can extract more than **59.3%** of this power. The maximum extractable power is:
$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.593 \times \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Solar Energy Incident on a Horizontal Surface (Conceptual)

The **solar constant** $G_{sc}$ is the energy flux perpendicular to the solar beam just outside Earth's atmosphere.

**Step 1:** Energy received by a surface of area $A$ in time $t$ (perpendicular to beam):
$$E_{\perp} = G_{sc} \times A \times t$$

**Step 2:** For a tilted surface at angle $\theta$ from the beam normal, the effective area is $A \cos\theta$:
$$E_{tilted} = G_{sc} \times A \cos\theta \times t$$

**Step 3:** After atmospheric attenuation, multiply by an **air-mass factor** (AM = 1 at zenith):
$$E_{ground} = G_{sc} \times A \times \cos\theta \times \tau^{AM}$$
where $\tau$ is atmospheric transmittance ($\approx 0.7$ for clear sky).

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A hydro-electric plant operates with a head of 50 m and a discharge of 30 m³/s. The overall plant efficiency is 80%. Calculate the power generated.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.
$$P = \eta \, \rho \, g \, h \, Q$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 50$ m, $Q = 30$ m³/s, $\eta = 0.80$.
$$P = 0.80 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 30$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate step-by-step.
$$P = 0.80 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1500$$
$$P = 0.80 \times 1000 \times 14715$$
$$P = 11,772,000 \text{ W} = 11.772 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4:** Final answer with units.
$$\boxed{P = 11.772 \text{ MW} \approx 11.8 \text{ MW}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This 11.8 MW plant can power approximately **8,000–10,000 households** (assuming 1.2–1.5 kW per home).

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Energy Resources Comparison (KTU Board View)

| Parameter | Coal | Oil | Natural Gas | Nuclear | Solar | Wind | Hydro |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State** | Solid | Liquid | Gas | Solid (U) | Radiation | Kinetic | Gravitational |
| **Reserves (yrs)** | ~150 | ~50 | ~55 | ~100 | ∞ | ∞ | Site-specific |
| **CO₂ Emission** | High | High | Medium | Zero (op) | Zero | Zero | Zero (op) |
| **Conversion Eff.** | 35–42% | 25–38% | 35–45% | 33% | 15–22% | 35–45% | 85–95% |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Very High | High | High | Very High |
| **Pollution** | High | High | Medium | Radioactive | Low | Low | Displacement |
| **Availability (India)** | Abundant | Imported | Limited | Limited Thorium | Excellent | Excellent | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Conversion Chain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Source Energy] --> B1[Converter Prime Mover]
    B1 --> C1[Working Fluid / Medium]
    C1 --> D1[Turbine / Engine]
    D1 --> E1[Generator]
    E1 --> F1[Electrical Energy]
    F1 --> G1[End Use Load]
    G1 --> H1[Waste Heat Losses]
    H1 --> I1[Environment]

    A1:::src
    H1:::waste
    I1:::waste

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef waste fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Energy flows left-to-right from **source** to **end-use**, while waste heat is rejected to the **environment** at every stage.

## 4.2 Classification of Energy Resources (Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Energy Resources] --> B0[By Origin]
    A0 --> C0[By Renewability]
    A0 --> D0[By Commercial Use]

    B0 --> B1[Solar]
    B0 --> B2[Geothermal]
    B0 --> B3[Tidal]
    B0 --> B4[Fossil]

    C0 --> C1[Renewable]
    C0 --> C2[Non-Renewable]

    C1 --> C1a[Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal, Hydrogen]
    C2 --> C2a[Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium]

    D0 --> D1[Conventional]
    D0 --> D2[Non-Conventional]

    D1 --> D1a[Coal, Oil, Gas, Hydro, Nuclear, Firewood]
    D2 --> D2a[Solar PV, Wind, OTEC, MHD, Fuel Cells, Tidal]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Primary Energy Source] --> Q1[Energy Capture / Collection System]
    Q1 --> R1[Energy Conversion Subsystem]
    R1 --> S1[Power Conditioning Unit]
    S1 --> T1[Distribution Network]
    T1 --> U1[End Use Application]

    R1 -.->|Losses| W1[Thermal Waste]
    R1 -.->|Losses| W2[Mechanical Friction]
    S1 -.->|Losses| W3[Electrical Losses]

    W1 --> E1[Environment]
    W2 --> E1
    W3 --> E1
```

**Engineering significance:** Every loss block represents an **irreversibility** in the thermodynamic sense. Minimizing these losses is the core aim of energy engineering and management.

## 4.4 Solar PV Conversion Pathway (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Sun Photons] --> S2[PV Cell Semiconductor]
    S2 --> S3[DC Electrical Output]
    S3 --> S4[Inverter DC to AC]
    S4 --> S5[AC Bus]
    S5 --> S6[Load / Grid]
    S5 -.->|Surplus| S7[Battery Storage]

    S2 -.->|Heat Loss| SW1[Thermal]
    S4 -.->|Switching Loss| SW2[Heat]
    SW1 --> ENV[Environment]
    SW2 --> ENV
```

**Key insight:** A PV cell directly converts **radiant energy → electrical energy**, bypassing the thermal/mechanical stages and thus avoiding the Carnot limit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define energy. List any four different forms of energy with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Energy is the capacity of a system to do work or transfer heat. It is a conserved scalar physical quantity measured in Joules (SI).

**Four forms of energy (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Kinetic Energy** — Energy of motion. Example: Wind blowing a turbine, moving water in a river.
2. **Potential Energy** — Stored energy due to position. Example: Water stored in a reservoir at height.
3. **Chemical Energy** — Energy stored in molecular bonds. Example: Coal, gasoline, food.
4. **Electrical Energy** — Energy of moving charges. Example: Current in a circuit, lightning.

*(Valid alternatives: Thermal, Nuclear, Radiant, Sound.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy resources. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Resources that are naturally replenished at a rate faster than consumption | Resources that exist in finite quantity and take millions of years to form |
| **Examples (1 mark)** | Solar energy, Wind energy, Hydel, Biomass, Tidal | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Uranium |
| **Replenish Time (1 mark)** | Continuous / Annual cycle | Geological time scales (~10⁶ years) |
| **Environmental Impact** | Low carbon footprint | High CO₂ & pollutants |
| **Availability** | Practentially inexhaustible | Limited reserves |

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1]`**

**(a)** Explain the various forms of energy with suitable examples. Discuss the principle of energy conversion with reference to a thermal power plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify energy resources in detail. Discuss the global and Indian energy scenario with relevant statistics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q1(a) — Forms of Energy & Thermal Power Plant (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Energy exists in multiple interconvertible forms. The principle of energy conversion states that energy can be transformed from one form to another but the total energy remains conserved (First Law of Thermodynamics).

**Forms of Energy (4 marks — ½ mark each, 8 forms):**

1. **Mechanical Energy (KE + PE):** KE = ½mv²; PE = mgh. *Example:* Water in a dam.
2. **Thermal Energy:** Heat content of a body. *Example:* Steam from a boiler.
3. **Chemical Energy:** Stored in molecular bonds. *Example:* Coal, petroleum.
4. **Electrical Energy:** Flow of electrons. *Example:* Current in a wire.
5. **Nuclear Energy:** Binding energy of nucleus. *Example:* Fission of U-235.
6. **Radiant Energy:** Electromagnetic radiation. *Example:* Sunlight.
7. **Sound Energy:** Mechanical vibration. *Example:* Acoustic waves.
8. **Mass Energy:** $E = mc^2$. *Example:* Matter-antimatter annihilation.

**Thermal Power Plant — Energy Conversion (2 marks):**

The conversion chain is:
$$\text{Chemical (Coal)} \xrightarrow{\text{combustion}} \text{Thermal (Steam)} \xrightarrow{\text{nozzle}} \text{Kinetic (Steam jet)} \xrightarrow{\text{turbine}} \text{Mechanical (Rotation)} \xrightarrow{\text{generator}} \text{Electrical (AC)}$$

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
Every stage has thermodynamic losses. The overall plant efficiency of a coal-fired thermal plant is typically **30–40%**, with the rest lost as waste heat in condenser and flue gases.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q1(b) — Classification of Energy Resources & Global/Indian Scenario (7 marks)

**A. Classification of Energy Resources (4 marks):**

**1. By Renewability (2 marks):**
- *Renewable:* Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Wave, OTEC, Biomass, Geothermal, Hydrogen.
- *Non-Renewable:* Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium).

**2. By Conventional Use (1 mark):**
- *Conventional:* Coal, Oil, Gas, Firewood, Large Hydro.
- *Non-Conventional:* Solar PV, Wind, MHD, Fuel Cells, OTEC, Tidal, Biomass gasification.

**3. By Stage of Use (1 mark):**
- *Primary:* Direct from nature (coal, sun, wind, uranium).
- *Secondary:* Converted from primary (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).

**B. Global & Indian Energy Scenario (3 marks):**

- **Global mix (2023):** Oil ~31%, Coal ~27%, Gas ~24%, Renewables ~13%, Nuclear ~4%, Hydro ~1%. (1 mark)
- **India's position:** 3rd largest energy consumer; 2nd largest importer of oil & coal. Installed capacity ~430 GW (2024), with renewables ~190 GW. (1 mark)
- **India's targets:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; Net-zero by 2070. (1 mark)

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]`**

**(a)** State and explain the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. How do they govern energy conversion processes? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydroelectric power plant has a head of 80 m and a discharge of 50 m³/s. The overall efficiency is 85%. Calculate:
   (i) The input power available from the water.
   (ii) The electrical power output.
   (iii) The energy generated per day in kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q2(a) — First & Second Laws (7 marks)

**First Law of Thermodynamics (3 marks):**

*Statement:* Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system is constant.

*Mathematical form:*
$$\Delta E_{system} = Q - W$$
where $Q$ = heat supplied to the system, $W$ = work done **by** the system, and $\Delta E$ = change in total energy (internal + kinetic + potential).

*Application to energy conversion:* It ensures that the **energy balance** in any conversion device (e.g., boiler, turbine, pump) is always closed. No device can produce more energy than the input.

**Second Law of Thermodynamics (3 marks):**

*Kelvin-Planck Statement:* It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and converting it all to work. (i.e., **no 100% efficient heat engine**).

*Clausius Statement:* Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body.

*Carnot Efficiency (upper limit):*
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$
where temperatures are in Kelvin.

*Application:* It governs the **direction** of energy flow and sets the **upper limit on efficiency** of any thermal conversion (e.g., a steam plant with $T_H = 800$ K and $T_C = 300$ K has $\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$).

**Governance of Conversion Processes (1 mark):**
The First Law tells us *how much* energy we get (quantity), while the Second Law tells us *how much useful work* we can extract (quality/exergy). Together they define the **maximum possible efficiency** of any energy system.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q2(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** Head $h = 80$ m, Discharge $Q = 50$ m³/s, Efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Input power from water (2 marks):**

Formula:
$$P_{input} = \rho g h Q$$

Substitution:
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 80 \times 50$$

Stepwise evaluation:
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4000$$
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 39,240$$
$$\boxed{P_{input} = 39,240,000 \text{ W} = 39.24 \text{ MW}}$$

**(ii) Electrical power output (2 marks):**

$$P_{output} = \eta \times P_{input} = 0.85 \times 39.24 \text{ MW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{output} = 33.354 \text{ MW} \approx 33.35 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Energy generated per day in kWh (3 marks):**

Time per day: $t = 24$ hours.

$$E = P_{output} \times t = 33.354 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h}$$

Convert MW to kW: $33.354 \text{ MW} = 33,354 \text{ kW}$.

$$E = 33,354 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h}$$
$$\boxed{E = 800,496 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 8.0 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often leave power in **W** when the question asks for **MW or kW**. Always re-check final units.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency (–2 marks):** In numerical problems, output = $\eta \times$ input. Skipping $\eta$ gives a wrong answer.
> 3. **Wrong temperature scale in Carnot (–2 marks):** Use **Kelvin**, not Celsius. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
> 4. **Confusing definitions of "renewable" vs "non-conventional" (–1 mark):** They are NOT synonyms. **Hydro is renewable + conventional. Nuclear is non-renewable + non-conventional.**
> 5. **Skipping the "chain" in thermal plant (–1 mark):** Always write the **complete conversion chain** (Chemical → Thermal → Kinetic → Mechanical → Electrical) for full marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula for KE/PE (–1 mark):** $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ (not $mv^2$). $PE = mgh$ (not $mgh^2$).

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Energy Conversion & Energy Resources**

- ✅ **Energy** = capacity to do work. SI unit = **Joule (J)**. 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J**.
- ✅ **8 primary forms** of energy: Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Electrical, Nuclear, Radiant, Electrochemical, Mass-energy.
- ✅ **Mass-energy equivalence:** $E = mc^2$ (Einstein, 1905).
- ✅ **First Law:** Energy is conserved. $\Delta E = Q - W$.
- ✅ **Second Law:** Entropy of universe increases. **No 100% efficient heat engine.**
- ✅ **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**).
- ✅ **Renewable ≠ Non-Conventional.** Nuclear = Non-Renewable + Non-Conventional.
- ✅ **Wind power** is proportional to $v^3$ — doubling wind speed gives **8× power**.
- ✅ **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².
- ✅ **Hydroelectric Power:** $P = \eta \rho g h Q = 9810 \, \eta \, h \, Q$ (kW, with $\rho = 1000$, $g = 9.81$).
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** Max wind turbine efficiency = **59.3%** = 16/27.
- ✅ India is the **3rd largest** energy consumer and **2nd largest** oil importer globally.
- ✅ **Thermal power plant chain:** Chemical → Thermal → Kinetic → Mechanical → Electrical.
- ✅ **PV cell** directly converts **Radiant → Electrical** (no thermal/mechanical stage).
- ✅ **Energy content of fuel** = mass × calorific value ($CV$ in J/kg or kCal/kg).
- ✅ Always quote the **First Law (conservation)** AND **Second Law (efficiency limit)** together when asked about energy conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global and Indian Scenario of Energy Resources and Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global and Indian Scenario of Energy Resources and Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Resources** are natural or processed substances, systems, or phenomena from which useful mechanical, thermal, electrical, or chemical energy can be extracted, converted, and utilized to drive socio-economic, industrial, and domestic activities. They are broadly classified as **Primary Energy Resources** (occurring in nature, e.g., coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind) and **Secondary Energy Resources** (derived from primary sources through conversion, e.g., electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).

> [!NOTE]
> **Global Energy Scenario** refers to the systematic assessment of the worldwide availability, production, distribution, consumption patterns, reserves, and policy frameworks of various energy carriers. The **Indian Energy Scenario** is the corresponding national-level analysis, contextualized by population, GDP, geography, indigenous reserves, and import dependency.

The unit used to compare different energy carriers on a common footing is the **Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** where:
$$\text{1 TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh} = 1.428 \text{ tce (Tonne of Coal Equivalent)}$$

The internationally accepted aggregated unit is the **Exajoule (EJ)**, where:
$$\text{1 EJ} = 10^{18} \text{ J} = 10^{9} \text{ GJ} = 23.88 \text{ MTOE}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Energy "Bloodstream" of an Economy

Think of an economy as a **living organism**:
- **Fossil fuels and uranium** behave like **stored fat reserves** — energy-dense, finite, and slowly depleted when the body uses them intensively.
- **Renewables (sun, wind, hydro, biomass)** act like a **daily meal** — replenished naturally every day, but must be harvested continuously because the "stomach" cannot store it indefinitely.
- **The power grid and oil pipelines** are the **blood vessels** that transport this energy.
- **Per capita energy consumption** is a measure of how much "blood flow" every individual citizen receives — a direct indicator of quality of life and industrial activity.

Just as a healthy body needs a balanced diet (not 100% fat, not 100% salad), a **resilient energy mix** must balance reliability, affordability, sustainability, and security — this is the essence of the global and Indian energy transition debate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (IEA & BP Statistical Review):** Global primary energy consumption is reported in **Exajoules (EJ)** or **Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent (MTOE)**. India's consumption is reported in the same units, allowing direct comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Global Energy Resources

### 2.1.1 Non-Renewable (Conventional / Fossil) Resources
1. **Coal** — Solid fossil fuel; dominant in India for power generation.
2. **Crude Oil (Petroleum)** — Liquid hydrocarbon; transport sector lifeblood.
3. **Natural Gas** — Cleanest fossil fuel; transitioning fuel in the energy mix.
4. **Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium)** — High energy density; low operational emissions.

### 2.1.2 Renewable (Non-Conventional) Resources
1. **Solar Energy** — Photovoltaic (PV) + Concentrated Solar Power (CSP).
2. **Wind Energy** — Onshore + Offshore.
3. **Hydropower** — Large, small, micro, and pumped storage.
4. **Biomass & Biogas** — Agricultural residues, municipal solid waste.
5. **Geothermal Energy** — Tapped in tectonically active regions.
6. **Tidal & Wave Energy** — Oceanic forms.

## 2.2 Global Energy Consumption Pattern

The world's primary energy mix (approximate shares, BP/IEA 2023-2024):

| Source | Approx. Global Share | Key Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Oil | **~31%** | Transport, petrochemicals |
| Coal | **~27%** | Power generation (China, India) |
| Natural Gas | **~24%** | Power, industry, heating |
| Renewables (incl. hydro) | **~14%** | Decarbonization push |
| Nuclear | **~4%** | Baseload power (France, Russia) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single largest consumer is **China (~25% of global energy)**, followed by the **USA (~16%)** and **India (~7%)**. Together, these three nations account for nearly half of all primary energy consumed on Earth.

## 2.3 Indian Energy Scenario

India is the **3rd largest primary energy consumer** and the **3rd largest electricity producer** globally. Salient features:

- **Coal-dominated** generation mix (historically >70% of electricity).
- **High import dependency**: ~85% of crude oil and ~50% of natural gas are imported.
- **Rapidly expanding renewable sector** (4th largest in installed RE capacity).
- **Low per-capita consumption** compared to global average → signals huge growth potential.
- **Ambitious targets** under the Paris Agreement (NDC): 500 GW non-fossil installed capacity by 2030; net-zero by 2070.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Engineering Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for Module-1 numerical questions in KTU ESE.

| Parameter | Formula / Identity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Energy conversion (Oil) | $\text{1 TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ | TOE / GJ / MWh |
| Energy conversion (Coal) | $\text{1 Tonne coal} \approx 25.8 \text{ GJ} \approx 0.614 \text{ TOE}$ | tce / GJ |
| Exajoule to MTOE | $1 \text{ EJ} = 23.88 \text{ MTOE}$ | EJ / MTOE |
| Barrel of Oil Equivalent | $\text{1 BOE} \approx 0.146 \text{ TOE} \approx 6.12 \text{ GJ}$ | BOE / GJ |
| kWh to Joule | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$ | kWh / J |
| Per Capita Consumption | $\text{PCC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Population}}$ | TOE/person |
| Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P) | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Years |
| Import Dependency | $\text{ID} = \dfrac{\text{Imports}}{\text{Apparent Consumption}} \times 100$ | % |
| Energy Share (Mix) | $\text{Share}_{i} = \dfrac{E_{i}}{\sum_{i} E_{i}} \times 100$ | % |
| Growth Rate (CAGR) | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{E_{\text{final}}}{E_{\text{initial}}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> Always report absolute values in **standard SI** (J) and large aggregates in **EJ or MTOE**. Mixing units (e.g., kWh for one source and TOE for another) is a **common KTU valuation mistake** — always normalize first.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the global and Indian energy scenario is **mission-critical** for:
1. **Power system planners** (forecasting demand, sizing generation mix).
2. **Policy makers** (subsidy design, energy security strategy).
3. **Industrial managers** (fuel-switching decisions, ESG reporting).
4. **Civil & Mechanical engineers** (HVAC sizing, boiler efficiency, plant design).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstration 1 — Energy Unit Conversion

**Problem:** A thermal power station consumes **2,50,000 tonnes of coal** in one year. The calorific value (GCV) of the coal is **4500 kcal/kg**. Convert this annual energy input into **(a) GJ**, **(b) MTOE**, and **(c) MWh**.

### Step 1 — Convert tonnes of coal to kilograms
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{coal}} &= 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ t} \times 1000 \text{ kg/t} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute total chemical energy in kcal
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{kcal}} &= M_{\text{coal}} \times \text{GCV} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ kg} \times 4500 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
&= 1.125 \times 10^{12} \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert kcal → kJ → GJ
Using the identity $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \text{ kJ}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{kJ}} &= 1.125 \times 10^{12} \times 4.184 \\
&= 4.707 \times 10^{12} \text{ kJ} \\
Q_{\text{GJ}} &= 4.707 \times 10^{12} \text{ kJ} \times 10^{-6} \text{ GJ/kJ} \\
&= 4.707 \times 10^{6} \text{ GJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert GJ → TOE → MTOE
Using $\text{1 TOE} = 42 \text{ GJ}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TOE} &= \dfrac{4.707 \times 10^{6}}{42} \\
&\approx 1.121 \times 10^{5} \text{ TOE} \\
\text{MTOE} &= 1.121 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 0.1121 \text{ MTOE}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert GJ → MWh
Using $\text{1 MWh} = 3.6 \text{ GJ}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MWh} &= \dfrac{4.707 \times 10^{6}}{3.6} \\
&\approx 1.3075 \times 10^{6} \text{ MWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (a)** $\approx 4.71 \times 10^{6}$ GJ    **Final Answer (b)** $\approx 0.1121$ MTOE    **Final Answer (c)** $\approx 1.31 \times 10^{6}$ MWh

---

## 3.2 Demonstration 2 — Per Capita Energy Consumption

**Problem:** A country consumed **900 MTOE** of primary energy in a year with a population of **140 crore**. Compute the per-capita consumption in **(a) TOE/person** and **(b) GJ/person**.

### Step 1 — Convert population to a workable number
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Population} &= 140 \text{ crore} = 140 \times 10^{7} = 1.4 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert total consumption from MTOE to TOE
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 900 \text{ MTOE} = 900 \times 10^{6} \text{ TOE} = 9.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ TOE}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute per-capita TOE
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCC}_{\text{TOE}} &= \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ TOE}}{1.4 \times 10^{9}} \\
&\approx 0.643 \text{ TOE/person}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert TOE/person to GJ/person
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCC}_{\text{GJ}} &= 0.643 \times 42 \\
&\approx 27.0 \text{ GJ/person}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (a)** $\approx 0.643$ TOE/person    **Final Answer (b)** $\approx 27.0$ GJ/person. For context, the world average is ~1.7 TOE/person and the USA is ~6.8 TOE/person — this country has significant development headroom.

---

## 3.3 Demonstration 3 — Import Dependency Ratio

**Problem:** A nation consumed **220 MTOE** of crude oil in a year, of which **40 MTOE** was produced domestically. Compute the **Import Dependency Ratio (IDR)**.

### Step 1 — Compute imports
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Imports} &= \text{Consumption} - \text{Domestic Production} \\
&= 220 - 40 = 180 \text{ MTOE}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the IDR formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDR} &= \dfrac{\text{Imports}}{\text{Consumption}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{180}{220} \times 100 \\
&\approx 81.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** IDR $\approx$ **81.82%**. A high IDR signals severe **energy security risk** and exposure to global crude price shocks — exactly the situation India faces.

---

## 3.4 Demonstration 4 — Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

**Problem:** A country has **proved coal reserves of 110 billion tonnes** and an **annual production of 800 million tonnes**. How many years will the reserves last at the current production rate?

### Step 1 — Apply R/P formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R/P} &= \dfrac{110 \times 10^{9} \text{ t}}{800 \times 10^{6} \text{ t/yr}} \\
&= \dfrac{110000}{800} \\
&\approx 137.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** R/P $\approx$ **137.5 years**. India has among the highest coal R/P ratios globally — a strategic advantage, but also a transition challenge, since locking into coal for a century is environmentally untenable.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Compatible Quick Comparison

| Metric | Global | India | Remark for Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary energy consumption (2023) | $\approx 620$ EJ | $\approx 38$ EJ | India = ~6% of world |
| Coal share in power mix | $\sim 36\%$ | $\sim 50\%$ | Higher in India |
| Crude oil import dependency | varies | $\sim 85\%$ | Strategic concern |
| Gas import dependency | varies | $\sim 50\%$ | LNG exposure |
| Per-capita energy (TOE/person) | $\sim 1.7$ | $\sim 0.65$ | Growth potential |
| Installed power capacity | $\sim 8{,}500$ GW | $\sim 430$ GW | India = 5% of global |
| Renewable installed share | $\sim 30\%$ | $\sim 33\%$ | India ahead of world avg |
| NDC target year | varies | Net-zero by 2070 | UNFCCC commitment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Global Energy Resources (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    A0["GLOBAL ENERGY RESOURCES"] --> B0["NON RENEWABLE CONVENTIONAL"]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Flow from Source to End-Use

```mermaid

    C1 --> S1["SECONDARY ENERGY ELECTRICITY HYDROGEN GASOLINE DIESEL"]

## 4.3 India's Installed Power Capacity Composition (Block Schematic)

```mermaid

    subgraph A["TOTAL INSTALLED CAPACITY INDIA APPROX 430 GW"]
        direction TB
