        R1["RENEWABLES SOLAR WIND BIOMASS APPROX 33 PERCENT"] --> SUM1
        H1["LARGE HYDRO APPROX 10 PERCENT"] --> SUM1
        N1["NUCLEAR APPROX 2 PERCENT"] --> SUM1
        B1["BALANCE OTHERS APPROX 5 PERCENT"] --> SUM1
    end
    SUM1 --> F1["FUTURE TARGET 500 GW NON FOSSIL BY 2030 NET ZERO BY 2070"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above intentionally use **plain uppercase text inside quoted labels** to remain compatible with all Mermaid renderers (GitHub, VSCode preview, and KTU-supported markdown viewers). Avoid using `**bold**` or `|pipe|` characters inside node labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **"Per Capita Energy Consumption"**. State the world average and India's per-capita primary energy consumption. Why is this parameter considered a key indicator of economic development?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Per Capita Energy Consumption is defined as the average primary energy used by each inhabitant of a region per year, given by:
$$
\text{PCC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Population}}
$$
World average is approximately **1.7 TOE/person/year**; India is around **0.65 TOE/person/year**. It is a key development indicator because energy underpins every productive activity — agriculture, industry, transport, healthcare, and digital services — so a higher PCC correlates strongly with higher HDI and GDP per capita.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **primary** and **secondary** energy resources, giving **two examples** of each. Why is electricity classified as a secondary energy carrier?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Primary energy resources occur in nature and can be extracted directly (e.g., **coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium, natural gas**). Secondary energy resources are derived from primary sources through conversion (e.g., **electricity, gasoline, diesel, hydrogen, steam**). Electricity is a secondary energy carrier because it is **generated** by converting mechanical (turbine), chemical (battery), solar (PV), or other forms — it does not exist as a free, extractable resource in nature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Nov 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice Set]**

### Option (i) — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat classification, describe the **global energy resources** available on Earth. Discuss the relative shares of coal, oil, natural gas, renewables, and nuclear in the present world energy mix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. Energy resources are classified as **Renewable (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal)** and **Non-Renewable (Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear Uranium/Thorium)**.
2. **Coal (~27%)** is the most-abundant fossil fuel; dominant in power generation in China, India, USA, and Australia. Solid, energy-dense (~25.8 GJ/tonne), high emissions.
3. **Oil (~31%)** is the largest single source; dominant in transport and petrochemicals. Barrel (bbl) = 159 L; 1 BOE ≈ 6.12 GJ.
4. **Natural Gas (~24%)** is the cleanest fossil (lowest CO₂/kWh among fossils); key transition fuel.
5. **Renewables incl. hydro (~14%)** — fastest-growing segment; solar PV alone added >400 GW globally in 2023.
6. **Nuclear (~4%)** — provides baseload low-carbon power; 442 reactors worldwide (IAEA 2024 data).

The global primary energy mix is moving slowly from fossil-dominated toward a low-carbon, renewables-led structure, though fossil fuels still account for ~80%.

---

**Q. (b)** Describe in detail the **Indian energy scenario**, highlighting the country's installed power capacity, fuel-wise generation share, import dependency, and the targets under the **Paris Agreement (NDC)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Installed capacity (~430 GW as of 2024)**: Thermal (coal + gas) ~50%, Renewables (solar, wind, biomass) ~33%, Large Hydro ~10%, Nuclear ~2%, others ~5%.
2. **Fuel-wise generation share** of electricity (2023-24): Coal ~73%, Gas ~6%, Hydro ~10%, Solar ~7%, Wind ~5%, Nuclear ~3%, Biomass/Biogas ~2%. Coal still dominates actual generation because thermal plants have higher **Plant Load Factor (PLF)**.
3. **Import dependency**: Crude oil ~85% (~85% of requirement is met through imports), Natural gas ~50%, Coal ~20-25% (coking coal nearly 100% imported).
4. **Key concerns**: Volatile global prices, forex outflow, energy security.
5. **NDC targets under Paris Agreement**: Reduce GDP emission intensity by 45% from 2005 levels by 2030; achieve **500 GW of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030**; create **2.5-3 Gt CO₂eq additional carbon sink** through forests; and reach **net-zero by 2070**.
6. **Strategic initiatives**: National Solar Mission, Green Hydrogen Mission, FAME-II (EVs), Saubhagya (rural electrification), UJALA (LED distribution).

---

### Option (ii) — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio**. Compute the R/P ratio for the following: Coal reserves = **120 billion tonnes**, annual production = **900 million tonnes**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
The R/P ratio represents the **number of years** the remaining proven reserves would last if production continues at the current rate, with no new discoveries.
$$
\text{R/P} = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}
$$

Substituting values:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R/P}_{\text{coal}} &= \dfrac{120 \times 10^{9} \text{ t}}{900 \times 10^{6} \text{ t/yr}} \\
&= \dfrac{120{,}000}{900} \\
&\approx 133.3 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** This is a very high R/P ratio, comparable to India's national figure. While this provides **long-term energy security for coal**, it also implies that the country must progressively transition away from coal to meet climate commitments. A high R/P ratio is therefore a **double-edged sword**: security now, transition challenge later.

---

**Q. (b)** A city consumes **1,20,000 TOE** of primary energy per year. Its **electrical energy consumption is 3,50,000 MWh/yr**. If the average plant efficiency for electricity generation is **35%**, compute: **(i)** the primary energy used for electricity, **(ii)** the share of electricity-related primary energy in the city's total, and **(iii)** the per-capita energy if the population is **15 lakh**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Primary energy for electricity:**
Electrical output = 3,50,000 MWh = $3.5 \times 10^5$ MWh.
Converting to GJ: $1 \text{ MWh} = 3.6 \text{ GJ}$, so:
$$
E_{\text{el, out}} = 3.5 \times 10^{5} \times 3.6 = 1.26 \times 10^{6} \text{ GJ}
$$
Accounting for 35% efficiency:
$$
E_{\text{el, in}} = \dfrac{1.26 \times 10^{6}}{0.35} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ GJ}
$$

**(ii) Convert city's total primary energy to GJ for comparison:**
$$
1.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ TOE} \times 42 \text{ GJ/TOE} = 5.04 \times 10^{6} \text{ GJ}
$$
Share:
$$
\text{Share}_{\text{el}} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^{6}}{5.04 \times 10^{6}} \times 100 \approx 71.43\%
$$

**(iii) Per-capita energy:**
$$
\text{PCC} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{5} \text{ TOE}}{15 \times 10^{5}} = 0.80 \text{ TOE/person/yr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (i) **3.6 × 10⁶ GJ**   (ii) **≈ 71.43%**   (iii) **0.80 TOE/person/yr**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason students lose marks.** Always convert **everything to a common unit (GJ or TOE)** before adding, comparing, or computing ratios.
> 2. Many students confuse **MTOE with TOE**, **EJ with GJ**, and **BOE with TOE**. The conversion identities in Section 2.4 must be **memorized verbatim**.
> 3. In R/P ratio questions, **forget to convert million tonnes to tonnes** (multiply by $10^6$) — this single error loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. In per-capita problems, **"crore" = $10^7$ and "lakh" = $10^5$**. Misreading this loses full credit.
> 5. In share-of-mix questions, **sum of all shares must be ~100%** — if your answer is far off, a unit or arithmetic error is almost certainly present.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Primary energy** = found in nature (coal, oil, gas, sun, wind, uranium). **Secondary energy** = derived by conversion (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).
- **1 TOE = 42 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 1.428 tce**. **1 EJ = 23.88 MTOE**. **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**.
- **Global energy mix (approx):** Oil 31% > Coal 27% > Gas 24% > Renewables 14% > Nuclear 4%.
- **Largest consumers:** China > USA > India. India = 3rd largest primary energy consumer globally.
- **India's installed power capacity (~430 GW):** Thermal ~50%, Renewables ~33%, Hydro ~10%, Nuclear ~2%, Others ~5%.
- **Import dependency:** Crude oil ~85%, Natural gas ~50%, Coking coal ~100% — strategic energy security challenge.
- **Per capita consumption:** World ~1.7 TOE; India ~0.65 TOE — growth headroom.
- **R/P ratio** = proven reserves ÷ annual production (in years). India's coal R/P ~140 years.
- **Import Dependency Ratio (IDR)** = (Imports ÷ Apparent Consumption) × 100.
- **Share in mix** = (Energy from source $i$ ÷ Total energy) × 100.
- **NDC targets (India, Paris Agreement):** 45% emissions intensity cut (vs 2005) by 2030; 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; Net-Zero by 2070.
- **Key Indian policy initiatives:** National Solar Mission, Green Hydrogen Mission, FAME-II, UJALA, Saubhagya, Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme.
- **Transition fuels:** Natural gas and nuclear (in the short term), green hydrogen (in the long term), solar/wind (rapidly scaling).
- **Energy security** is the **3-E framework**: Energy security, Energy equity (access), Environmental sustainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute formula flashcards (write this on your hand, literally):**
> - $\text{1 TOE} = 42$ GJ
> - $\text{1 kWh} = 3.6$ MJ
> - $\text{1 EJ} = 23.88$ MTOE
> - $\text{PCC} = E_{\text{total}} / N$
> - $\text{IDR} = (I / C) \times 100$
> - $\text{R/P} = R / P$ (years)
> - $\text{Share}_i = (E_i / \sum E_i) \times 100$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impact of Energy on Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Impact of Energy on Economy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impact of Energy on Economy** refers to the systematic, multi-dimensional influence that the availability, accessibility, affordability, and quality of energy resources exert on macroeconomic indicators such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP), industrial output, employment, trade balance, inflation, and overall human development indices of a nation. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is examined through three foundational pillars: 1. **Energy–GDP Nexus** – The empirical and theoretical relationship between national energy throughput and economic output. 2. **Energy Intensity (EI)** – A measure of the energy required to produce one unit of economic value. 3. **Energy Elasticity** – The sensitivity of energy demand relative to economic growth. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** The standard metric used universally to quantify this relationship is **Energy Intensity = Primary Energy Consumption (in Mtoe) ÷ GDP (in constant USD or INR)**, with a global benchmark tracked by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the economy of a country as a **living human body**. - **Energy** is the **blood** that circulates through the body (the economy). - **Industries, transport, agriculture, and households** are the **organs** that require this blood to function. - **GDP** is analogous to the **pulse rate and overall vitality** of the body. If blood supply (energy) is cut off or becomes impure (unaffordable/unsustainable), the organs fail — factories shut down (industry collapses), trucks stop moving (transport halts), farmers cannot irrigate (agriculture drops), and people freeze or overheat (loss of welfare). The body (economy) collapses. Conversely, a healthy, oxygenated, well-circulated blood supply (clean, affordable, reliable energy) keeps every organ performing optimally, allowing the body to grow stronger, run faster, and live longer (sustainable economic growth). > [!TIP] > **Real-world example:** During the 1973 OPEC Oil Crisis, oil prices quadrupled. Countries like the USA, UK, and Japan saw their GDP growth rate drop by 2–4%, inflation soar, and unemployment rise — a textbook demonstration of energy directly damaging economic health. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024 - It forms the **conceptual foundation** for the rest of the course (energy management, audit, conservation, renewables). - It directly maps to **CO1 (Understand the global and national energy scenario and its economic implications)**. - It is a **high-frequency short-answer and essay topic** in KTU End Semester Examinations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Energy–Economy Feedback Loop The relationship is **bidirectional**, not one-way: **Forward Path (Energy → Economy):** - Energy availability enables industrialization → increased manufacturing output → higher GDP. - Reliable electricity supports service sector (IT, banking, telecom) → GDP growth. - Affordable cooking/lighting fuel raises human productivity → better labour output. **Feedback Path (Economy → Energy):** - Higher GDP → higher purchasing power → greater demand for vehicles, appliances, air conditioners → higher energy demand. - Economic growth requires infrastructure (roads, factories, ports) which are energy-intensive to construct. > [!NOTE] > This bidirectional causality is why energy is termed the **"master resource"** in macroeconomic literature. ### 2.2 Key Theoretical Concepts | # | Concept | Explanation | Engineering/Economic Significance | |---|---------|-------------|-----------------------------------| | 1 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | Energy consumed per unit GDP | Indicates energy efficiency of an economy | | 2 | **Energy Elasticity (ε)** | % change in energy ÷ % change in GDP | Predicts future energy demand from GDP forecasts | | 3 | **Energy Security** | Uninterrupted availability at affordable price | Determines import-export trade balance | | 4 | **Energy Poverty** | Lack of access to modern energy services | Directly correlates with low HDI | | 5 | **Embodied Energy** | Energy used to manufacture a product | Used in life-cycle costing of goods | | 6 | **Energy Subsidy** | Government financial support to reduce energy cost | Impacts fiscal deficit and market distortion | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following four formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this module.** | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Description of Variables | Typical Units | |--------------|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $$EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$$ | $E_{total}$ = total primary energy consumed; GDP = Gross Domestic Product | Mtoe per billion USD (or kgoe per USD) | | **Energy Elasticity (ε)** | $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\% \Delta E}{\% \Delta GDP}$$ | $\%\Delta E$ = percentage change in energy; $\%\Delta GDP$ = percentage change in GDP | Dimensionless (pure number) | | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $$CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ | $V_f$ = final value; $V_i$ = initial value; $n$ = number of years | Fraction (× 100 for %) | | **Energy Demand Forecast** | $$E_{future} = E_{present} \times (1 + \varepsilon \cdot g)^t$$ | $\varepsilon$ = elasticity; $g$ = GDP growth rate; $t$ = years into future | Same as $E_{present}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key economic insights embedded in the formulas:** > - If $\varepsilon > 1$ → economy is **energy-intensive** (developing economies). > - If $\varepsilon < 1$ → economy is **energy-efficient** (post-industrial service economies). > - If $\varepsilon \to 0$ → economy is **decoupled** from energy (best long-term goal). ### 2.4 Energy Intensity in India: A Real-World Snapshot According to IEA data (for context only, do not cite as a specific year): - **India's EI** has been historically around **0.20 kgoe/USD** (PPP adjusted), higher than the global average of ~0.18. - This indicates India still has **significant scope for energy efficiency improvement** in industrial, agricultural, and residential sectors. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility The formulas above are used by: - **Planning Commission / NITI Aayog** of India for long-term energy demand projections. - **International Energy Agency (IEA)** in its annual *World Energy Outlook* report. - **Power utility companies** for load forecasting and capacity expansion planning. - **Corporate sustainability teams** to compute Scope 1, 2, 3 carbon footprints. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The "KTU Favourite" Numerical > [!NOTE] > This is the **exact type of 7-mark problem** KTU examiners love to set. Mastering this solution guarantees marks. **Problem Statement:** > A developing country had the following macroeconomic indicators: > - **GDP in 2019:** ₹ 150 lakh crore (constant 2015 prices) > - **GDP in 2024:** ₹ 195 lakh crore (constant 2015 prices) > - **Primary Energy Consumption in 2019:** 850 Mtoe > - **Primary Energy Consumption in 2024:** 970 Mtoe > > **Calculate:** > (i) Average annual GDP growth rate (CAGR) > (ii) Average annual energy consumption growth rate (CAGR) > (iii) Energy Elasticity of the country > (iv) Energy Intensity in 2019 and 2024 > (v) Comment on whether the economy is moving toward energy efficiency. --- #### Part (i): Average Annual GDP Growth Rate (CAGR) **Step 1 — Identify the variables.** $$GDP_{2019} = 150 \text{ lakh crore}, \quad GDP_{2024} = 195 \text{ lakh crore}, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the CAGR formula.** $$g_{GDP} = \left(\frac{GDP_{2024}}{GDP_{2019}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ $$g_{GDP} = \left(\frac{195}{150}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1$$ **Step 3 — Compute the ratio.** $$g_{GDP} = (1.30)^{0.2} - 1$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the power using logarithms.** $$\ln(1.30) = 0.2624$$ $$0.2624 \times 0.2 = 0.05248$$ $$e^{0.05248} = 1.0539$$ **Step 5 — Final value.** $$g_{GDP} = 1.0539 - 1 = 0.0539$$ **Step 6 — Express as a percentage.** $$\boxed{g_{GDP} \approx 5.39\% \text{ per annum}}$$ --- #### Part (ii): Average Annual Energy Consumption Growth Rate (CAGR) **Step 1 — Identify variables.** $$E_{2019} = 850 \text{ Mtoe}, \quad E_{2024} = 970 \text{ Mtoe}, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the CAGR formula.** $$g_{E} = \left(\frac{E_{2024}}{E_{2019}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ $$g_{E} = \left(\frac{970}{850}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1$$ $$g_{E} = (1.1412)^{0.2} - 1$$ **Step 3 — Compute logarithmically.** $$\ln(1.1412) = 0.1320$$ $$0.1320 \times 0.2 = 0.0264$$ $$e^{0.0264} = 1.0268$$ **Step 4 — Final value.** $$g_{E} = 1.0268 - 1 = 0.0268$$ **Step 5 — Express as a percentage.** $$\boxed{g_{E} \approx 2.68\% \text{ per annum}}$$ --- #### Part (iii): Energy Elasticity (ε) **Step 1 — Compute total percentage change in GDP over 5 years.** $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{GDP_{2024} - GDP_{2019}}{GDP_{2019}} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{195 - 150}{150} \times 100 = 30.00\%$$ **Step 2 — Compute total percentage change in Energy over 5 years.** $$\%\Delta E = \frac{E_{2024} - E_{2019}}{E_{2019}} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta E = \frac{970 - 850}{850} \times 100 = 14.12\%$$ **Step 3 — Apply the elasticity formula.** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{14.12}{30.00}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.47}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative cross-check using growth rates:** $\varepsilon \approx g_E / g_{GDP} = 2.68 / 5.39 = 0.497$ — both methods give consistent values in the **0.45–0.50** range. The small difference arises from the choice of average vs total growth basis. --- #### Part (iv): Energy Intensity (EI) for 2019 and 2024 **Step 1 — Compute EI for 2019.** $$EI_{2019} = \frac{E_{2019}}{GDP_{2019}} = \frac{850 \text{ Mtoe}}{150 \text{ lakh crore}}$$ $$EI_{2019} = 5.667 \text{ Mtoe per lakh crore}$$ **Step 2 — Compute EI for 2024.** $$EI_{2024} = \frac{E_{2024}}{GDP_{2024}} = \frac{970 \text{ Mtoe}}{195 \text{ lakh crore}}$$ $$EI_{2024} = 4.974 \text{ Mtoe per lakh crore}$$ --- #### Part (v): Engineering Interpretation / Comment | Parameter | 2019 | 2024 | Trend | |-----------|------|------|-------| | GDP (lakh crore ₹) | 150 | 195 | ↑ +30% | | Energy (Mtoe) | 850 | 970 | ↑ +14.12% | | Energy Intensity | 5.667 | 4.974 | ↓ −12.2% | | Energy Elasticity | — | 0.47 | < 1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner-ready comments (write at least 3 lines in the exam):** > 1. The economy is growing at **5.39% per annum** while energy is growing only at **2.68% per annum** → the country is **partially decoupled** from energy. > 2. Since $\varepsilon = 0.47 < 1$, the economy is **moving toward energy efficiency**. > 3. Energy intensity has **dropped by 12.2%** in 5 years, indicating successful adoption of efficient technologies, structural shift from industry to services, and/or policy interventions. > 4. To continue this trend, the country should invest in **renewables, energy-efficient industrial processes, and demand-side management**. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Framework | Observation | Diagnosed State | Recommended Policy | |-------------|-----------------|--------------------| | $\varepsilon > 1.2$ | Energy-intensive, vulnerable economy | Mandate energy audits, industrial retrofitting | | $0.8 < \varepsilon < 1.2$ | Normal growth phase | Continue current mix, plan for grid expansion | | $\varepsilon < 0.8$ | Efficient, mature economy | Push for renewables, electrification of transport | | $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Fully decoupled (rare) | Maintain efficiency standards; export the model | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Energy–Economy Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Primary Energy Sources
Coal Oil Gas Nuclear Hydro Solar Wind] --> B[Energy Conversion
Power Plants Refineries] B --> C[Energy Distribution
Grid Pipelines] C --> D[End-Use Sectors
Industry Transport Agriculture Residential Commercial] D --> E[Economic Output
GDP Industrial Output Employment Exports] E --> F[Macroeconomic Indicators
GDP per Capita HDI Trade Balance Inflation] F --> G[Policy and Investment Decisions] G --> A D -.Feedback.-> A E -.Feedback.-> B ``` ### 4.2 Diagram: Energy Elasticity Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compute Energy Elasticity epsilon] --> B{epsilon value} B -- Greater than 1.2 --> C[Energy Intensive Economy] B -- Between 0.8 and 1.2 --> D[Normal Coupling] B -- Less than 0.8 --> E[Efficient Economy] B -- Approximately 0 --> F[Fully Decoupled Economy] C --> G[Action: Mandate efficiency programs] D --> H[Action: Balanced energy mix planning] E --> I[Action: Promote renewables and EVs] F --> J[Action: Maintain standards and export know-how] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram: Components of Energy Intensity Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Energy Intensity Reduction Pathways] --> B[Technological Pathway] A --> C[Structural Pathway] A --> D[Behavioral Pathway] B --> B1[Efficient Motors] B --> B2[LED Lighting] B --> B3[High-Efficiency Boilers] B --> B4[Smart Grids] C --> C1[Shift from Industry to Services] C --> C2[Move from Heavy to Light Manufacturing] C --> C3[Digitalization of Processes] D --> D1[Public Awareness] D --> D2[Energy Labels] D --> D3[Behavioral Nudges and Tariffs] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Drawing such simple **block diagrams** (3–5 boxes with arrows) for an essay-type answer can fetch you **1–2 extra marks** for visual presentation, as recommended by KTU's OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define Energy Intensity. Mention its units and explain its significance as a macroeconomic indicator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Energy Intensity is defined as the amount of primary energy consumed per unit of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Mathematically, > $$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{GDP}}$$ > > **Units (0.5 mark):** Mtoe per billion USD (in international comparisons) or kgoe per USD in PPP terms. > > **Significance (1 mark):** A lower EI indicates a more energy-efficient economy. Countries with high EI (e.g., China, Russia) are considered energy-intensive, while those with low EI (e.g., Japan, UK, Switzerland) have more efficient, service-oriented economies. EI is used by IEA and World Bank to rank countries and track sustainability progress. #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **What is meant by Energy Elasticity? Mention its typical range for a developing economy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Energy Elasticity ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of the percentage change in energy demand to the percentage change in GDP. > $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Energy}}{\%\Delta \text{GDP}}$$ > > **Typical Range (1 mark):** For a developing economy, $\varepsilon$ is typically between **0.8 and 1.2** (sometimes up to 1.5 in early industrial phases). For a developed, post-industrial economy, $\varepsilon$ falls to **0.3–0.6**. > > **Interpretation (0.5 mark):** $\varepsilon > 1$ means economic growth is leading to disproportionately higher energy demand — a sign of an energy-intensive economy. $\varepsilon < 1$ indicates partial decoupling of growth from energy consumption. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination pattern, every Module-1 question for 14 marks carries an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are provided below. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style] **(a)** Discuss in detail the relationship between energy consumption and economic development. Highlight the role of energy in industrialisation, agriculture, and the service sector. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a suitable example, explain how fluctuations in energy prices impact the macroeconomic indicators of a country. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Energy Consumption and Economic Development (7 marks):** 1. **Industrialization (2.5 marks):** Energy is the backbone of industrial production. Steel, cement, chemicals, textiles, and manufacturing sectors require uninterrupted electrical and thermal energy. Historical evidence shows that during the Industrial Revolution, the UK consumed 1 million tons of coal in 1800; by 1900, it had risen to 250 million tons, coinciding with its economic dominance. A 10% rise in industrial energy supply typically correlates with a 6–8% rise in industrial output in developing economies. 2. **Agriculture (2 marks):** Mechanized farming, irrigation pumps, fertilizer production, cold storage, and food processing all require energy. India's Green Revolution (1960s–70s) was powered by subsidized electricity for tube wells, raising food grain output from 72 million tonnes (1965) to 176 million tonnes (1990). Energy access transforms subsistence agriculture into commercial agriculture. 3. **Service Sector (2.5 marks):** The modern service economy — IT, banking, telecommunications, healthcare, education — depends critically on **reliable electricity**. India's IT-BPM sector, contributing ~7% of GDP, requires 24×7 power. According to industry estimates, a 1% rise in power outage frequency reduces IT firm productivity by ~0.3%. Hence, energy quality (not just quantity) is essential for the tertiary sector. --- **(b) Impact of Energy Price Fluctuations (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Direct Cost-Push Inflation (2.5 marks):** A rise in crude oil price raises the cost of transportation, manufacturing, and feedstock for petrochemicals. This propagates through the economy as cost-push inflation. Example: When global crude oil prices rose from $30/barrel (2014) to $115/barrel (June 2022), India's WPI inflation rose to 15.88% in May 2022. **Step 2 — Trade Balance Deterioration (2 marks):** India imports ~85% of its crude oil requirement. A $10/barrel rise increases the import bill by ~$15 billion annually, widening the Current Account Deficit (CAD) and putting downward pressure on the rupee. **Step 3 — Reduced Consumer Purchasing Power (1.5 marks):** Higher fuel and electricity tariffs reduce disposable income, suppressing demand for non-essential goods, slowing GDP growth. **Step 4 — Monetary Policy Response (1 mark):** Persistent energy-driven inflation forces the central bank to raise interest rates, which in turn reduces investment and slows capital formation. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion (must be written to fetch full marks):** Energy price stability is a precondition for macroeconomic stability. Countries that diversify their energy mix (renewables + nuclear + domestic gas) and build strategic petroleum reserves are better insulated from external price shocks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice] **(a)** Define Energy Elasticity. A country's GDP grew from ₹ 100 lakh crore to ₹ 125 lakh crore over 5 years, while its energy consumption increased from 800 Mtoe to 920 Mtoe. Calculate the energy elasticity and interpret the result. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of renewable energy in promoting sustainable economic development. List at least four techno-economic benefits. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute % change in GDP.** $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{125 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 25\%$$ **Step 2 — Compute % change in Energy.** $$\%\Delta E = \frac{920 - 800}{800} \times 100 = 15\%$$ **Step 3 — Apply elasticity formula.** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{15}{25} = 0.60$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (must write 3 lines for full marks).** Since $\varepsilon = 0.60 < 1$, the country exhibits **partial decoupling** of energy consumption from economic growth. For every 1% increase in GDP, energy demand rises by only 0.6%, indicating a moderately energy-efficient economy. To achieve full decoupling, the country must invest in renewables, energy-efficient technologies, and structural shifts toward the service sector. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Many students write the inverse ratio ($\%\Delta GDP / \%\Delta E$) — that is wrong! Elasticity is always $\%\Delta E \div \%\Delta GDP$. **Memorize the order: Energy in numerator, GDP in denominator.** --- **(b) Role of Renewable Energy in Sustainable Economic Development (7 marks):** 1. **Employment Generation (2 marks):** The renewable energy sector is more labour-intensive per MW installed compared to fossil fuels. IRENA estimates that renewable energy jobs globally have grown to over 13 million, with solar PV being the largest employer. 2. **Energy Security (1.5 marks):** Domestic renewable resources (sun, wind, biomass) reduce dependence on imported fuels, improving the trade balance and insulating the country from global price volatility. 3. **Decentralized Power (1.5 marks):** Solar mini-grids and rooftop systems can electrify remote rural areas, boosting agricultural productivity, small-scale industries, and educational outcomes. 4. **Environmental Co-Benefits (1.5 marks):** Renewables reduce CO₂, SO₂, and particulate emissions, lowering healthcare costs and avoiding climate-related economic losses. India has committed to 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030, supporting its net-zero 2070 target. 5. **Foreign Investment Attraction (0.5 mark):** Green Hydrogen and offshore wind attract ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) capital from international markets. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Formula Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up Energy Intensity and Energy Elasticity formulas. **Remember:** Intensity uses **absolute values** (E/GDP), Elasticity uses **percentage changes** (%ΔE / %ΔGDP). > 2. **No Units Mentioned (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always specify units (Mtoe, kgoe, billion USD). KTU examiners explicitly award a unit-mark in numericals. > 3. **CAGR Misuse (–1 mark):** CAGR and simple average growth are different. Use CAGR for multi-year data. > 4. **Lack of Interpretation (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme values "analysis and interpretation" highly. After every numerical, write **at least 3 lines** of interpretation in plain English. > 5. **No Diagrams (–0.5 mark):** Adding a small block diagram or pie chart shows OBE-aligned holistic understanding. > 6. **Missing Conclusion (–0.5 mark):** Always end essay-type answers with a 1–2 line summary/conclusion. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 2 hours before the exam):** - ✅ **Energy Intensity** = Total Energy ÷ GDP. Lower EI = more efficient economy. - ✅ **Energy Elasticity** = %ΔE ÷ %ΔGDP. If ε < 1, economy is partially decoupled. - ✅ **CAGR formula** is the most-tested numerical tool in this module. - ✅ **Four major macro-indicators affected by energy:** GDP, Inflation, Trade Balance, Employment. - ✅ **Energy–Economy relationship is bidirectional:** Energy enables growth; growth drives energy demand. - ✅ **Three pillars of energy's economic impact:** Availability, Affordability, Reliability. - ✅ **Energy Security** = Uninterrupted supply at affordable price, free from geopolitical disruption. - ✅ **India's EI** is higher than the global average → significant scope for efficiency improvement. - ✅ **OPEC 1973, 1979, 1990 Gulf War, 2022 Russia-Ukraine** are key historical events to cite for energy-economy linkage. - ✅ **Renewables** = jobs + energy security + decarbonization + rural electrification. - ✅ Always write **units, formulae, and interpretation** — not just final answers. - ✅ For 14-mark answers, follow the structure: **Definition → Explanation → Example → Diagram → Conclusion**. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Impact of Energy on Economy**

topics.title: Energy Policies, Development and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Policies, Development and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Energy–Development–Environment Nexus

**Energy Policy** is a formalized set of governmental directives, statutes, incentives, and regulations that dictate how a nation produces, distributes, prices, and consumes energy resources. It functions as the strategic bridge between **economic development** (the pursuit of higher GDP, industrialization, and improved quality of life) and **environmental sustainability** (the preservation of ecosystems, climate stability, and biodiversity).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, this topic establishes the *macro-economic and ecological framework* within which every future module — renewable technologies, energy auditing, and conservation — must operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *Energy Policy is the deliberate mechanism through which a government balances the trilemma of Energy Security, Energy Equity (access & affordability), and Environmental Sustainability to drive socio-economic development.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Legged Stool"

Imagine a three-legged stool where each leg represents one pillar:
- **Leg 1 — Development:** Provides the thrust for industry, jobs, and human welfare. Needs energy as "blood."
- **Leg 2 — Energy:** The "blood" itself, sourced from coal, oil, gas, nuclear, or renewables.
- **Leg 3 — Environment:** The "earth" that supports everything; if poisoned, the stool collapses.

Energy Policy is the **carpenter** that ensures all three legs are cut to the *right length* — too much focus on development and the environment leg cracks (climate change); too much on environment and development starves (energy poverty).

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Definition | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Security** | Uninterrupted availability of energy at an affordable price | National priority |
| **Energy Independence** | Meeting domestic demand primarily from indigenous sources | Strategic goal |
| **Energy Intensity** | Energy consumed per unit of GDP (toe/million $) | Efficiency metric |
| **Energy Elasticity** | Ratio of % growth in energy demand to % growth in GDP | Planning parameter |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Total GHG emissions (in **tCO₂e**) directly/indirectly caused by an activity | Environmental metric |
| **Sustainable Development** | Development that meets present needs without compromising future generations | Brundtland definition, 1987 |
| **Climate Change** | Long-term shift in global/regional climate patterns attributed to GHG emissions | UNFCCC framework |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:**
> The global warming potential (GWP) of **CO₂ = 1**, **CH₄ = 28**, **N₂O = 265** (over 100 years, IPCC AR5 values). These are the conversion factors examiners frequently test in carbon-equivalent calculations.

---

## 1.3 The Global Energy Trilemma (WEC Framework)

The **World Energy Council's Energy Trilemma** is a critical conceptual model for KTU:

```
        ENERGY SECURITY
              ▲
             /│\
            / │ \
           /  │  \
          /   │   \
         /    │    \
        /     │     \
       /      │      \
      ●───────┼───────●
ENVIRONMENTAL        ENERGY
SUSTAINABILITY        EQUITY
```

A balanced policy must simultaneously address all three vertices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of Energy Policy

A modern energy policy is constructed from **five operational pillars**:

1. **Resource Strategy** — Domestic vs. imported, fossil vs. renewable mix.
2. **Pricing & Subsidies** — Market-based tariffs, cross-subsidization, fossil fuel subsidies.
3. **Regulation & Standards** — Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT), Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC), BEE star labeling.
4. **R&D and Innovation** — Public investment in cleantech, hydrogen, storage.
5. **International Cooperation** — UNFCCC, IEA, IRENA, bilateral treaties.

### 2.2 The Energy–Development Linkage

The relationship between economic growth and energy consumption is described by the **Kaya Identity** (1989), which is a *must-study* identity for KTU:

$$\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Population
- $G/P$ = GDP per capita (affluence)
- $E/G$ = Energy intensity of the economy
- $\text{CO}_2/E$ = Carbon intensity of the energy supply

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight:** The Kaya Identity tells us that CO₂ emissions can be decoupled from economic growth by acting on **E/G** (efficiency) or **CO₂/E** (cleaner fuels) — this is the theoretical basis for India's **Panchamrit** commitment at COP-26.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** $= \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{GDP}$ | $E$ = energy, GDP in constant \$ | toe / million \$ | National efficiency comparison |
| 2 | **Energy Elasticity ($\varepsilon$)** $= \dfrac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | $E$ = energy consumption | dimensionless | Long-term demand forecasting |
| 3 | **Per Capita Energy** $= \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{Population}}$ | $E$ = total energy | toe/person | Standard of living indicator |
| 4 | **Kaya Identity** $\text{CO}_2 = P \cdot (G/P) \cdot (E/G) \cdot (\text{CO}_2/E)$ | As above | GtCO₂/yr | Emission decomposition |
| 5 | **Carbon Intensity of GDP** $= \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions}}{GDP}$ | CO₂ output | kgCO₂/\$ | Decarbonization tracking |
| 6 | **Diversification Index (HHI)** $= \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i^2$ | $s_i$ = share of source $i$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Measures import/mix concentration |
| 7 | **Energy Return on Investment (EROI)** $= \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{invested}}}$ | Net useful energy | dimensionless | Resource viability |
| 8 | **GWP-Weighted Emissions** $= \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | $m_i$ = mass of gas | tCO₂e | Unified carbon accounting |

> [!NOTE]
> **HHI Insight:** A value of $1.0$ means **100 % dependence on a single source** (e.g., oil); lower is better. India’s HHI for primary energy ≈ **0.34** (moderately diversified).

## 2.4 Indian Energy Policy Landscape — Chronology

| Year | Policy / Mission | Key Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **2002** | **NEP (National Electricity Policy)** | Power for all, open access |
| **2005** | **National Mission on Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** | PAT scheme for industries |
| **2006** | **National Tariff Policy** | Rational pricing |
| **2008** | **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)** | 8 national missions |
| **2010** | **Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** | 100 GW solar by 2022 |
| **2014** | **Make in India** | Manufacturing-led growth |
| **2015** | **UDAY Scheme** | DISCOM financial turnaround |
| **2016** | **Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana (UDAY)** | AT&C loss reduction |
| **2017** | **Saubhagya Scheme** | 100 % household electrification |
| **2021** | **National Hydrogen Mission** | Green hydrogen ecosystem |
| **2022** | **Panchamrit / LiFE** | Net-zero by 2070 |
| **2023** | **Green Credit Programme** | Market-based environmental incentives |

### 2.5 The Eight Missions Under NAPCC

1. **National Solar Mission**
2. **National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)**
3. **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat**
4. **National Water Mission
5. **National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem
6. **National Mission for a Green India
7. **National Mission for Sustainable Agriculture
8. **National Mission on Strategic Knowledge for Climate Change**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy policy decisions directly drive:
- **Power-plant design** (CEA clearance norms, emission standards for SO₂, NOₓ, PM).
- **Industrial boiler selection** (PAT cycle targets).
- **Building HVAC sizing** (ECBC compliance).
- **Vehicle selection** (BS-VI norms, EV FAME-II subsidies).
- **Manufacturing choices** (carbon border adjustment mechanism — CBAM in EU).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always link a policy with a *technology consequence*. For example, **PAT → adoption of VFDs and waste-heat recovery in cement plants**. Such "policy-to-tech" linkages fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Decoupling CO₂ from GDP via Kaya Decomposition

**Problem:** India's data (approximate, 2022):
- $P = 1.42 \times 10^9$ people
- GDP per capita = \$2,400
- Energy intensity $E/G = 0.20$ kgoe/\$
- Carbon intensity of energy $\text{CO}_2/E = 1.65$ tCO₂/toe

**Find:** Total CO₂ emissions using Kaya Identity, and the % reduction needed in $E/G$ to meet a target of 30 % emissions cut by 2030 (assuming GDP grows 6 %/yr, population grows 0.8 %/yr).

### Step 1: Apply the Kaya Identity

$$
\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}
$$

Substituting the given values (converting to consistent units — energy in toe, GDP in \$):

$$
\text{CO}_2 = (1.42 \times 10^9) \times (2400) \times (0.20) \times (1.65)
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
1.42 \times 10^9 \times 2400 = 3.408 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
3.408 \times 10^{12} \times 0.20 = 6.816 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
6.816 \times 10^{11} \times 1.65 = 1.1246 \times 10^{12} \text{ tCO}_2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CO}_2 \approx 1.12 \text{ GtCO}_2 \text{ (current)}}
$$

### Step 2: Project 2030 Emissions Under BAU

Growth factors (compounded over 8 years from 2022 → 2030):

$$
P_{2030} = 1.42 \times 10^9 \times (1.008)^8 = 1.42 \times 10^9 \times 1.0654 = 1.513 \times 10^9
$$

$$
(G/P)_{2030} = 2400 \times (1.06)^8 = 2400 \times 1.5938 = 3825 \text{ \$/capita}
$$

Total GDP in 2030:

$$
G_{2030} = 1.513 \times 10^9 \times 3825 = 5.787 \times 10^{12} \text{ \$}
$$

BAU emissions (assuming $E/G$ and $\text{CO}_2/E$ unchanged):

$$
\text{CO}_{2,\text{BAU}} = 5.787 \times 10^{12} \times 0.20 \times 1.65 = 1.910 \text{ GtCO}_2
$$

### Step 3: Apply the 30 % Reduction Target

$$
\text{CO}_{2,\text{target}} = 0.70 \times 1.910 = 1.337 \text{ GtCO}_2
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Required Energy Intensity

Rearranging Kaya with target emission:

$$
(E/G)_{\text{new}} = \frac{\text{CO}_{2,\text{target}}}{G_{2030} \times (\text{CO}_2/E)}
$$

$$
(E/G)_{\text{new}} = \frac{1.337 \times 10^{12}}{5.787 \times 10^{12} \times 1.65} = 0.140 \text{ kgoe/\$}
$$

### Step 5: Compute % Reduction in Energy Intensity

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{0.20 - 0.140}{0.20} \times 100 = 30.0\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** To achieve a 30 % cut in absolute emissions, India must reduce its energy intensity by **30 % over 8 years** — i.e., a **3.75 % annual efficiency improvement** (compounded). This is precisely the engineering challenge the BEE, ECBC, and PAT schemes are designed to address.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Energy Elasticity for Demand Forecasting

**Problem:** Over the last decade, India's GDP grew at 7 %/yr and primary energy demand grew at 4.5 %/yr. Predict the energy demand in 2030 if 2022 demand was 850 Mtoe and GDP grows at 6 %/yr until 2030.

### Step 1: Calculate Current Energy Elasticity

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{4.5}{7.0} = 0.643
$$

### Step 2: Project GDP Growth Multiplier (2022 → 2030)

$$
\text{GDP multiplier} = (1.06)^8 = 1.5938
$$

### Step 3: Project Energy Growth

Annual energy growth rate:

$$
\% \Delta E_{\text{new}} = \varepsilon \times \%\Delta GDP = 0.643 \times 6 = 3.86\% \text{ /yr}
$$

### Step 4: Compute 2030 Energy Demand

$$
E_{2030} = 850 \times (1.0386)^8 = 850 \times 1.3544 \approx 1151 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An elasticity of **0.643 (< 1)** indicates India is in a phase of **relative decoupling** — energy grows slower than GDP, thanks to efficiency gains. Mature economies reach $\varepsilon \to 0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: HHI for Energy Mix Diversification

**Problem:** A state's electricity mix is — Coal 60 %, Gas 15 %, Hydro 10 %, Solar 10 %, Wind 5 %. Compute HHI and comment on diversification.

### Step 1: Compute Squared Shares

$$
(0.60)^2 = 0.3600
$$

$$
(0.15)^2 = 0.0225
$$

$$
(0.10)^2 = 0.0100
$$

$$
(0.10)^2 = 0.0100
$$

$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025
$$

### Step 2: Sum the Squared Shares

$$
HHI = 0.3600 + 0.0225 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 + 0.0025 = 0.4050
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** HHI between 0.15 – 0.25 = well diversified; 0.25 – 0.40 = moderately concentrated; **> 0.40 = highly concentrated**. India's state above is highly coal-dependent, justifying strong policy push for renewables.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Policy → Mechanism → Engineering Outcome

| Policy Lever | Economic Mechanism | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Feed-in Tariff (FiT) | Guaranteed price for 25 yrs | Boosts solar PV installation |
| RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) | Mandates % RE purchase | Forces DISCOMs to sign PPAs |
| PAT (Perform Achieve Trade) | Tradable ESCerts | Industry invests in VFDs, WHR |
| ECBC | Building code for new construction | Efficient HVAC, lighting retrofits |
| FAME-II | Subsidy on EV purchase | Higher EV adoption, lower ICE demand |
| Carbon Tax | Price on emissions | Switch from coal to gas/RE |
| Green Credit | Tradable environmental credits | Reforestation, sustainable farming |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
Define the term "Energy Trilemma" as conceptualized by the World Energy Council. List its three dimensions.

**Model Answer:**
The Energy Trilemma is a framework introduced by the **World Energy Council (WEC)** to assess national energy performance across three interlinked dimensions:
1. **Energy Security** — the effective management of primary energy supply (domestic + imported) to meet demand reliably.
2. **Energy Equity** — universal access to affordable, reliable energy for all sections of society.
3. **Environmental Sustainability** — reduction of GHG emissions and pollution from energy systems to combat climate change.

A well-balanced policy must optimize all three simultaneously.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
What is the **Kaya Identity**? Explain each of its four factors.

**Model Answer:**
The Kaya Identity is a mathematical equation proposed by **Yoichi Kaya (1989)** that decomposes total CO₂ emissions into four multiplicative drivers:

$$
\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}
$$

- **$P$** = Population
- **$G/P$** = GDP per capita (Affluence)
- **$E/G$** = Energy intensity of the economy
- **$\text{CO}_2/E$** = Carbon intensity of the energy mix

It is widely used by IPCC and policy analysts to attribute emission changes to demographic, economic, efficiency, and fuel-mix factors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Discuss the major **features of India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC, 2008)**. List and briefly describe any **four** of its eight national missions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **Kaya Identity**, explain how India can decouple its CO₂ emissions from economic growth. Support your answer with **one numerical example**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)** was released in **June 2008** by the Prime Minister's Council on Climate Change. It outlines a **multi-pronged, long-term strategy** to address climate change while ensuring developmental goals.

**Key features:**
1. **Eight National Missions** covering solar, energy efficiency, habitats, water, Himalayan ecosystem, green cover, agriculture, and knowledge.
2. **Dual focus** on adaptation and mitigation.
3. **Sustained economic growth** as a precondition for adaptation capacity.
4. **Technology development and transfer.**
5. **International cooperation** under UNFCCC.

**Four missions explained:**

| Mission | Objective |
|---|---|
| **National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** | 100 GW solar capacity by 2022; later raised to 300 GW RE by 2030 |
| **NMEEE (PAT Scheme)** | Mandate energy efficiency in energy-intensive industries; tradable ESCerts |
| **National Mission on Sustainable Habitat** | Promote urban transport, ECBC compliance, solid-waste management |
| **National Mission for a Green India** | Afforestation on 6 million hectares; increase forest cover to 33 % |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **Kaya Identity** states:

$$
\text{CO}_2 = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{\text{CO}_2}{E}
$$

To decouple emissions from growth, India must reduce either **$E/G$** (energy intensity) or **$\text{CO}_2/E$** (carbon intensity of energy), or both, while $G/P$ continues to rise.

**Numerical Example (from §3.1 above):**
- Current CO₂ ≈ 1.12 GtCO₂
- Projected 2030 BAU CO₂ ≈ 1.91 GtCO₂
- To meet 30 % reduction → energy intensity must fall from 0.20 to 0.14 kgoe/\$ (a 30 % cut).
- This 3.75 %/yr efficiency improvement is achievable through PAT, ECBC, FAME-II, and solar/wind capacity additions (reducing $\text{CO}_2/E$).

Hence, decoupling is mathematically and technically feasible.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Define **Energy Intensity** and **Energy Elasticity**. Calculate the **per-capita energy consumption** of a country with population 140 crore and total primary energy consumption of 870 Mtoe. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Paris Agreement (2015)** with reference to India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). Mention India's **Panchamrit** announcements at COP-26. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definitions:**
- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Energy consumed per unit of GDP.
$$
EI = \frac{E_{\text{consumption}}}{GDP} \quad [\text{toe per million \$}]
$$
- **Energy Elasticity ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of percentage change in energy demand to percentage change in GDP.
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}
$$

**Per-capita energy calculation:**

Given: $P = 140$ crore $= 1.4 \times 10^9$ persons, $E = 870$ Mtoe.

$$
E_{\text{per capita}} = \frac{870 \times 10^6 \text{ toe}}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 0.6214 \text{ toe/person}
$$

Converting to kgoe: $0.6214 \times 1000 = 621.4$ kgoe/person.

**Comment:** India's per-capita energy consumption (~0.62 toe) is **less than one-third of the world average (~1.8 toe)** and far below developed nations (US ≈ 7 toe). This reflects:
1. **Energy poverty** — many households lack access to modern fuels.
2. **Developmental deficit** — large section of population engaged in low-energy-intensity agriculture.
3. **Policy imperative** — justifies expansion of clean energy access through Saubhagya, UJALA, and PM-KUSUM.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Paris Agreement** adopted at **COP-21 in December 2015** is a legally binding international treaty on climate change with the central goal of:

> Holding the increase in global average temperature to **well below 2 °C** above pre-industrial levels and pursuing efforts to limit it to **1.5 °C**.

**Key pillars:**
- **NDCs (Nationally Determined Contributions):** Each country sets its own 5-year emission-reduction targets.
- **Global Stocktake:** Every 5 years, collective progress is reviewed.
- **Climate finance:** Developed nations pledged \$100 billion/yr (subsequently raised to \$300 bn by 2035 at COP-29).
- **Technology transfer and capacity building.**

**India's Updated NDC (Aug 2022):**
1. Reduce **GDP emission intensity by 45 %** by 2030 (from 2005 levels).
2. Achieve **50 % cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources** by 2030.
3. Create additional **carbon sink of 2.5–3 GtCO₂e** through forests and tree cover by 2030.
4. Achieve **net-zero by 2070**.

**India's Panchamrit (Five Nectar Elements), announced at COP-26, Glasgow (Nov 2021):**
1. Reach **500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity** by 2030.
2. Generate **50 % of energy from renewable sources** by 2030.
3. Reduce **total projected carbon emissions by 1 billion tonnes** by 2030.
4. Reduce **carbon intensity of the economy by 45 %** by 2030 (from 2005).
5. Achieve **net-zero emissions by 2070**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently **conflate "NDC" with "NAPCC"** — they are NOT the same. NDC is a *Paris Agreement* instrument submitted to UNFCCC; NAPCC is a *domestic* policy of 2008. Likewise, do not write "Panchamrit" as a treaty — it is India's *announcement* at COP-26, not a separate international agreement. Mixing these terms is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trilemma** = Security + Equity + Sustainability (WEC).
- **Kaya Identity** = $P \times (G/P) \times (E/G) \times (\text{CO}_2/E)$.
- **Energy Intensity** = Energy/GDP; **Per-capita energy** = Energy/Population.
- **Elasticity < 1** = decoupling; **> 1** = rebound-prone growth.
- **NAPCC (2008)** = 8 national missions; **NDC (Paris, 2015)** = 5-year emission targets.
- **India's Panchamrit** = 500 GW RE, 50 % non-fossil by 2030; **net-zero 2070**.
- **HDI ↔ Energy access** — energy is a precondition for human development.
- **Decarbonization levers:** efficiency ($E/G\downarrow$), cleaner fuels ($\text{CO}_2/E\downarrow$), CCUS, renewables.
- **International instruments:** UNFCCC (1992), Kyoto (1997), Paris (2015), Glasgow (2021).
- **HHI** for energy mix: < 0.25 well diversified; > 0.40 highly concentrated.
- **PAT, ECBC, RPO, FiT, FAME-II** are key Indian policy instruments you must be able to explain with *one example each*.
- Always quote **numeric targets** in answers (e.g., "100 GW solar by 2022," "net-zero 2070") — examiners reward specificity.
- For derivations, **show unit conversions explicitly** (toe ↔ kgoe ↔ GJ).
- In Part B, **frame every policy with an engineering consequence** to score the application marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PRIME-E":**
> **P**olicy (NAPCC/NDC) → **R**enewables (Solar/Wind/Hydrogen) → **I**ntensity (EI & Kaya) → **M**issions (8 NAPCC) → **E**missions (GWP, tCO₂e) — covers 90 % of Module-1 paper.

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module 1
