```mermaid
    PEMFC --> COMMON[Common: H2 Fuel + O2 Oxidant + DC Electricity + H2O + Heat]
    AFC --> COMMON
    PAFC --> COMMON
    MCFC --> COMMON
    SOFC --> COMMON
```

## 4.3 PEMFC Polarization Curve (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Circuit E Nernst 1.23 V]:::pt1 -->|Activation Loss| B[Steep Drop 0.85-1.0 V]:::pt2
    B -->|Ohmic Linear| C[Mid Region 0.65-0.75 V]:::pt3
    C -->|Concentration Cliff| D[Limiting Current Cutoff]:::pt4
    D --> E[Net Useful Power Window]:::pt5

    classDef pt1 fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000
    classDef pt2 fill:#ffe2b3,stroke:#a3560b,color:#000
    classDef pt3 fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef pt4 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#7a1f1f,color:#000
    classDef pt5 fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#5b2a8a,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Key Constructional Components of a PEMFC (Textual Schematic)

| Layer (Anode → Cathode) | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Anode Flow Plate (Graphite/Metal) | Distributes H₂ gas uniformly across active area |
| 2. Anode Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL — Carbon Paper) | Allows gas to reach catalyst, conducts electrons |
| 3. Anode Catalyst Layer (Pt nanoparticles on Carbon) | Splits H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ (HOR) |
| 4. **PEM (Nafion 117/115)** | Conducts H⁺ only; blocks electrons and gases |
| 5. Cathode Catalyst Layer (Pt) | Combines ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O (ORR) |
| 6. Cathode GDL | Removes product water; conducts electrons |
| 7. Cathode Flow Plate | Distributes air/O₂; collects current |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define a fuel cell. How is it different from a conventional battery?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A fuel cell is an **electrochemical device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (H₂) and an oxidant (O₂) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, as long as reactants are supplied.

| Aspect | Fuel Cell | Battery |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | External (continuous supply) | Internal (stored chemicals) |
| Recharging | Not required | Required after discharge |
| By-product | Water + Heat | Heat only |
| Operating mode | Open system | Closed system |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for any 1 valid difference.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Write the anode and cathode half-cell reactions of a PEMFC.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \quad E^{\circ} = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

**Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2O \quad E^{\circ} = +1.229 \text{ V}$$

**Overall:**
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

*(1 mark for anode, 1 mark for cathode, 1 mark for overall.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a PEMFC**. Discuss the role of the **Nafion membrane** and the **platinum catalyst**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A H₂/O₂ PEMFC operates at 80 °C with partial pressures $P_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 2$ atm, $P_{H_2O} = 0.4$ atm. Using the Nernst equation, calculate the **open-circuit cell voltage**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Construction (3 Marks):**
A single PEMFC consists of:
- **Anode flow plate** — distributes H₂.
- **Anode GDL + Catalyst layer** — Pt nanoparticles on carbon support.
- **PEM (Nafion)** — proton-conducting solid polymer (~50–200 µm).
- **Cathode catalyst + GDL.**
- **Cathode flow plate** — distributes O₂/air and removes product water.

**2. Working (3 Marks):**
- H₂ enters anode, dissociates on Pt: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$.
- $H^+$ ions migrate through the **hydrated Nafion membrane** to the cathode.
- Electrons are **forced through the external circuit** (doing useful work).
- At the cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$.
- Product water is swept out by excess air flow; heat is removed by coolant.

**3. Role of Nafion & Pt (1 Mark):**
- **Nafion:** A sulfonated tetrafluoroethylene polymer. Its $SO_3^-$ groups conduct $H^+$ but reject $e^-$, $O_2$, and $H_2$ — providing **selective ionic conduction**.
- **Pt Catalyst:** Lowers activation energy for both HOR and ORR; without Pt, kinetics are too sluggish at 80 °C.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
$T = 80 + 273 = 353$ K
$P_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 2$ atm, $P_{H_2O} = 0.4$ atm
$E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V, $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Nernst Equation:**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left( \frac{P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute RT/nF:**

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure ratio:**

$$Q = \frac{2.5 \times (2)^{1/2}}{0.4} = \frac{2.5 \times 1.4142}{0.4} = \frac{3.5355}{0.4} = 8.839$$

**Step 4 — Logarithm:**

$$\ln(8.839) = 2.179$$

**Step 5 — Voltage correction:**

$$\Delta E = 0.01521 \times 2.179 = 0.0331 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Final Voltage:**

$$E = 1.229 + 0.0331 = 1.2621 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{OCV} \approx 1.262 \text{ V}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Q.A)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Classify the major types of fuel cells. Compare **PEMFC** and **SOFC** in terms of electrolyte, mobile ion, operating temperature, efficiency, and applications. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A fuel cell stack delivers **200 A** at an average cell voltage of **0.65 V**. Calculate the **hydrogen consumption rate (in kg/hr)** and the **electrical power output (kW)**. Take M(H₂) = 2 kg/kmol. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Classification (3 Marks):**
- **PEMFC** — Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell.
- **AFC** — Alkaline Fuel Cell.
- **PAFC** — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell.
- **MCFC** — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell.
- **SOFC** — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell.

**2. Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | Solid ceramic (YSZ — ZrO₂–Y₂O₃) |
| Mobile Ion | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| Operating T | 60–100 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Electrical Efficiency | 40–60% | 50–60% |
| Power Range | 1 W – 250 kW | 1 kW – multi-MW |
| Applications | Cars, buses, portable | Stationary power, large plants |
| Startup Time | Seconds | Hours |
| Catalyst | Pt required | None (Ni cermet) |

**3. Key Distinction (1 Mark):**
SOFC's high temperature allows **internal reforming** of CH₄ to H₂ and CO, eliminating the need for an external reformer and a noble-metal catalyst.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $I = 200$ A
- $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V (assume single cell for simplicity, or average)
- $M_{H_2} = 2$ kg/kmol
- $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Step 1 — Hydrogen molar flow (using Faraday's law):**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{200}{192{,}970} = 1.0364 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kmol/s:**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = 1.0364 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kmol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times M = 1.0364 \times 10^{-9} \times 2 = 2.073 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/hr:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.073 \times 10^{-9} \times 3600 = 7.46 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Electrical power:**

$$P = V \times I = 0.65 \times 200 = 130 \text{ W} = 0.13 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 7.46 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/hr}, \quad P = 0.13 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing mobile ions** — In PEMFC, the mobile ion is $H^+$ (NOT $OH^-$). In SOFC, it is $O^{2-}$. Many students write the wrong one.
> 2. **Wrong Nernst exponent** — For the $O_2$ term, the exponent is **1/2** (because of $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ in the half-reaction), NOT 1.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in RT/nF** — If $T$ is in K and $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, the result is in **V** (because $J/C = V$). No conversion needed.
> 4. **Forgetting the 2 in $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2$** — It is the most common arithmetic slip.
> 5. **Marking electrodes backwards** — In a **galvanic** (power-producing) fuel cell, the **anode is negative**, the **cathode is positive**. Reverse this and you lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Quoting 83% as "fuel cell efficiency"** — That is the **thermodynamic maximum**. Always clarify "theoretical" vs "practical".
> 7. **Drawing PEMFC with water at anode** — Product water forms at the **cathode**, never at the anode.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Fuel cell = open system**; converts chemical → electrical directly (no combustion).
- ✅ **Standard reversible voltage** of H₂/O₂ cell: **E° = 1.229 V** at 25 °C.
- ✅ **Nernst equation** accounts for partial pressure and temperature effects.
- ✅ **PEMFC electrolyte:** Polymer membrane (Nafion) → conducts **H⁺**.
- ✅ **PEMFC operating T:** 60–100 °C → needs **Pt catalyst**.
- ✅ **SOFC electrolyte:** YSZ ceramic → conducts **O²⁻** at 600–1000 °C.
- ✅ **Anode reaction (PEMFC):** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ *(negative terminal)*.
- ✅ **Cathode reaction (PEMFC):** $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ *(positive terminal)*.
- ✅ **Polarization curve has 3 loss regions:** Activation, Ohmic, Concentration.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic efficiency η = ΔG/ΔH = 83% (H₂/HHV basis)**.
- ✅ **Practical efficiency:** 40–60% (PEMFC), 50–60% (SOFC).
- ✅ **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol**.
- ✅ **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- ✅ **Mobile ion memory aid:** **P**EMFC → **P**rotons (H⁺); **A**FC → h**A**droxyls (OH⁻); **PA**FC → **P**rotons (H⁺); **M**CFC → **M**oves Carbonate (CO₃²⁻); **S**OFC → **S**end **O**xide (O²⁻).
- ✅ **Product water in PEMFC appears at the cathode** — important for hydration of the Nafion membrane.
- ✅ **Fuel cell stack voltage** = N × V_cell, where N is the number of cells.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Management and Energy Audit - Energy Management: Definitions and Significance, Objectives, Characterizing of Energy Usage, Energy Management Program, Energy Strategies and Energy Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management (EM):** Energy Management is the systematic, proactive, and well-documented process of **planning, organizing, directing, and controlling** the procurement, conversion, distribution, and utilization of energy to meet organizational objectives — while optimizing cost, minimizing environmental impact, and ensuring sustainable operation.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India**, Energy Management is defined as:

> *"The strategy of adjusting and optimizing energy resources and energy conversion processes to attain a sustainable reduction in the specific energy consumption (SEC) of a system, plant, or organization."*

**Energy Audit (EA)** is the precursor and the verification tool for Energy Management — it is the systematic inspection, measurement, and analysis of energy flows in a system to identify conservation opportunities and quantify savings.

---

## 1.2 The 6 Pillars of Energy Management (KTU Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> Energy Management rests on **six interdependent pillars** prescribed under the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| # | Pillar | Meaning |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Commitment** | Top-level management pledge and policy declaration |
| 2 | **Planning** | Energy baseline, target-setting, and strategy formulation |
| 3 | **Implementation** | Deployment of programs, retrofits, and operating procedures |
| 4 | **Monitoring & Verification (M&V)** | Continuous tracking using Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) |
| 5 | **Evaluation** | Comparison of actual vs. predicted performance |
| 6 | **Review & Continual Improvement** | PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) loop re-iteration |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Bank Account"

Imagine your **factory is a household**, and **energy is your monthly salary**:

- **Income** → Energy entering the system (electricity, fuel, steam).
- **Expenses** → Useful work + Wasted losses (heat leaks, idle motors, leakages).
- **Budgeting** → Energy planning & load scheduling.
- **Auditing** → Monthly review of your expense slip.
- **Savings** → Reduction in Specific Energy Consumption (SEC).
- **Investment** → Capital expenditure on efficient technology (LEDs, VFDs, insulation).

> Just as a **CFO monitors cash flow**, an **Energy Manager monitors kWh and TOE (Tonnes of Oil Equivalent) flow**. The goal is **maximum output per unit energy** — analogous to *value-for-money* in finance.

---

## 1.4 Why Energy Management? — Significance in the Engineering Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance Statement (KTU Board-Standard):** "Energy Management is significant because energy is a **strategic, economic, and environmental resource**; unmanaged energy use erodes profit margins, inflates carbon footprints, and violates national regulations like the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (India)** and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme**."

The **"4-E" framework** summarizes significance:

1. **Economic** — Reduces operating cost; payback periods typically 1–3 years for retrofits.
2. **Environmental** — Lowers GHG emissions (kg CO₂ saved per MWh).
3. **Energy Security** — Reduces import dependence (India imports ~85% of its crude oil).
4. **Ethical / Regulatory** — Compliance with **ISO 50001:2018** and BEE's PAT scheme.

---

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Constants (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **1 TOE** = **41.868 GJ** = **11.63 MWh** (thermal)
> - **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** = **860 kcal**
> - **1 kcal** = **4.186 kJ**
> - **Standard coal equivalent**: 1 TCE = 7000 kcal/kg
> - **CO₂ emission factor (India grid)** ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂ per kWh**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Objectives of Energy Management — Hierarchical Decomposition

Energy Management objectives operate at **three hierarchical levels**:

### Tier 1 — Strategic (Top Management)
- Establish **Energy Policy** aligned with corporate vision.
- Allocate **capital budget** for energy projects.
- Set **long-term reduction targets** (e.g., 20% reduction in SEC over 5 years).

### Tier 2 — Tactical (Energy Manager / Middle Management)
- Conduct periodic **Energy Audits** (Walk-through → Detailed).
- Develop **Energy Management Program (EnMP)**.
- Train workforce and deploy ISO 50001 procedures.

### Tier 3 — Operational (Engineers / Floor Staff)
- Implement **housekeeping measures** (no leaks, no idle running).
- Maintain equipment at **design efficiency**.
- Report energy KPIs daily/weekly.

---

## 2.2 Characterizing Energy Usage — The "Know-Your-Load" Doctrine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before you can manage energy, you must first **characterize** (i.e., quantify and profile) how energy is being used.

Energy usage characterization involves **four analytical dimensions**:

### A. By Energy Form (Primary vs. Secondary)
- **Primary energy**: Coal, crude oil, natural gas, hydro, solar radiation.
- **Secondary energy**: Electricity, refined fuels, steam, hot water.
- **Final energy**: Energy reaching the end-user (post-transmission/distribution).
- **Useful energy**: Energy actually performing the desired task.

### B. By End-Use (Sectoral Split)
Typical industrial energy end-uses:
- Motor-driven systems (60–70%)
- Process heating (15–20%)
- Steam systems (10–15%)
- HVAC & lighting (5–10%)

### C. By Time (Load Curve Analysis)
- **Base Load**: 24×7 demand.
- **Peak Load**: Typically 6 PM–10 PM in India.
- **Load Factor (LF)** = Average Load / Peak Load.

### D. By Cost (Economic Value)
- **Avoided cost** vs. **Tariff structure** (kVAh, TOD, ToU).

---

## 2.3 The Energy Management Program (EnMP) — ISO 50001 Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Style):** An **Energy Management Program (EnMP)** is a documented plan that identifies, prioritizes, implements, and tracks energy performance improvement projects over a defined period.

### Standard EnMP Structure:

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Establish Energy Policy | Signed policy document |
| 2 | Conduct Energy Review | Baseline + EnPIs established |
| 3 | Identify SEUs (Significant Energy Uses) | Ranked list of high-impact areas |
| 4 | Set Objectives & Targets (O&T) | Quantified reduction goals |
| 5 | Develop Action Plans | Project sheets with CAPEX & payback |
| 6 | Implement & Operate | Commissioned retrofits |
| 7 | Monitor & Measure (M&V) | KPI dashboards |
| 8 | Review by Management | Management Review Minutes (MRM) |
| 9 | Continual Improvement | Updated EnMP version |

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these **eight core equations** — they cover ~90% of KTU numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **SEC = E ÷ P** | Specific Energy Consumption = Energy ÷ Production output | kWh/ton, MJ/kg, TOE/unit |
| 2 | **η = Useful Output Energy ÷ Input Energy** | Efficiency | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | **LF = Average Load ÷ Peak Load** | Load Factor | % |
| 4 | **PF = kW ÷ kVA** | Power Factor | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Savings = (SEC_old − SEC_new) × Annual Production** | Annual energy saved | kWh/yr, TOE/yr |
| 6 | **Payback = CAPEX ÷ Annual Monetary Savings** | Simple Payback Period | Years |
| 7 | **EROI = Energy Output ÷ Energy Input** | Energy Return On Investment | Ratio |
| 8 | **CO₂_saved = ΔEnergy × Emission Factor** | Avoided emissions | kg CO₂, tCO₂ |

> **Critical Notation Rule:** For absolute value or modulus in tables, use $\vert x \vert$ — **never** use the pipe symbol inside table rows.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These principles drive:
- **Industrial Energy Efficiency** in cement, steel, aluminium (PAT scheme).
- **Building Energy Management Systems (BEMS)** in commercial complexes.
- **Smart-grid demand response** programs at utility level.
- **ISO 50001 EnMS certifications** in manufacturing plants worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Production-Indexed Energy Intensity

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard):**
A steel plant consumes **2,400,000 kWh/month** of electrical energy and **150 TOE/month** of thermal energy to produce **10,000 tonnes/month** of finished steel. Determine the **combined SEC** in MJ/tonne.

### Step 1 — Convert Electrical Energy to MJ

$$
E_{elec} = 2{,}400{,}000 \, \text{kWh}
$$

Using the conversion $1 \, \text{kWh} = 3.6 \, \text{MJ}$:

$$
E_{elec,\text{MJ}} = 2{,}400{,}000 \times 3.6
$$

$$
E_{elec,\text{MJ}} = 8{,}640{,}000 \, \text{MJ}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Thermal Energy (TOE) to MJ

Given $1 \, \text{TOE} = 41.868 \, \text{GJ} = 41{,}868 \, \text{MJ}$:

$$
E_{therm,\text{MJ}} = 150 \times 41{,}868
$$

$$
E_{therm,\text{MJ}} = 6{,}280{,}200 \, \text{MJ}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Energy

$$
E_{total} = E_{elec} + E_{therm}
$$

$$
E_{total} = 8{,}640{,}000 + 6{,}280{,}200
$$

$$
E_{total} = 14{,}920{,}200 \, \text{MJ/month}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Combined SEC

$$
\text{SEC} = \frac{E_{total}}{\text{Production}}
$$

$$
\text{SEC} = \frac{14{,}920{,}200}{10{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SEC} = 1492.02 \, \text{MJ/tonne}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The world-class benchmark for integrated steel plants is **18–22 GJ/tonne** (18000–22000 MJ/tonne). A SEC of **1.49 GJ/tonne** indicates this is a **secondary steelmaking** (electric arc furnace) facility — consistent with the high electrical and lower thermal energy input.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Annual Cost Savings & Payback Period

**Problem Setup:**
A textile mill replaces **50 conventional motors** (each 10 kW, 90% efficient) with **IE4 premium-efficiency motors** (each 10 kW, 94% efficient). The motors run **6000 hr/year**. Electricity tariff = **₹7.50/kWh**. Motor capital cost premium = **₹8000/motor**.

### Step 1 — Calculate Input Power Loss (Old Motors)

$$
\eta_{old} = 0.90, \quad P_{shaft} = 10 \, \text{kW}
$$

$$
P_{in,old} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{old}} = \frac{10}{0.90}
$$

$$
P_{in,old} = 11.11 \, \text{kW/motor}
$$

$$
\text{Loss}_{old} = 11.11 - 10 = 1.11 \, \text{kW/motor}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Input Power Loss (New Motors)

$$
\eta_{new} = 0.94
$$

$$
P_{in,new} = \frac{10}{0.94} = 10.638 \, \text{kW/motor}
$$

$$
\text{Loss}_{new} = 10.638 - 10 = 0.638 \, \text{kW/motor}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Loss Reduction per Motor

$$
\Delta P_{loss} = 1.11 - 0.638
$$

$$
\Delta P_{loss} = 0.472 \, \text{kW/motor}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Annual Energy Savings (50 Motors)

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = \Delta P_{loss} \times N_{motors} \times \text{Hours}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 0.472 \times 50 \times 6000
$$

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 141{,}600 \, \text{kWh/year}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Annual Monetary Savings

$$
\text{Savings} = \Delta E_{annual} \times \text{Tariff}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 141{,}600 \times 7.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings} = \text{₹}10{,}62{,}000 \, \text{per year}}
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Payback Period

$$
\text{CAPEX}_{total} = 50 \times 8000 = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Payback} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{10{,}62{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback} = 0.377 \, \text{years} \approx 4.5 \, \text{months}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Payback < 1 year → **Highly bankable project**. Net Present Value (NPV) at 10% discount rate over 10 years would yield an IRR > 250%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Energy Strategies & Planning Choices

| Strategy Type | Implementation Horizon | Typical SEC Reduction | Capital Intensity |
|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Housekeeping** | 0–6 months | 2–5% | Very Low |
| **Low-cost retrofits** | 6–12 months | 5–15% | Low |
| **Equipment replacement** | 1–3 years | 10–25% | Medium |
| **Process redesign** | 3–5 years | 20–40% | High |
| **Cogeneration / Waste-heat recovery** | 2–5 years | 15–30% | High |
| **Renewable integration** | 5–10 years | 30–60% (long-term) | Very High |

---

## 3.4 The Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) Cycle Applied to Energy Management

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PLAN} &: \text{ Set energy policy, targets, action plans} \\
\text{DO} &: \text{ Implement training, retrofits, controls} \\
\text{CHECK} &: \text{ Measure \& compare against baseline (EnPI)} \\
\text{ACT} &: \text{ Standardize gains; revise targets upward}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **deming cycle** backbone of **ISO 50001:2018** and is a **guaranteed KTU board question** (either definition or schematic).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Management Program (EnMP) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Top Management Commitment] --> B[Define Energy Policy]
    B --> C[Establish Energy Baseline EnB]
    C --> D[Conduct Energy Review]
    D --> E[Identify Significant Energy Uses SEUs]
    E --> F[Set Objectives and Targets O&T]
    F --> G[Develop Action Plans]
    G --> H[Implement Energy Efficiency Measures]
    H --> I[Monitor Measure and Verify M&V]
    I --> J{Energy Performance Achieved?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Document and Standardize]
    J -- No --> L[Root Cause Analysis CAPA]
    L --> G
    K --> M[Management Review MRM]
    M --> N[Continual Improvement PDCA Loop]
    N --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the flow top-down:** The EnMP is a **closed-loop system**; it never terminates — it cycles back into the next planning horizon.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Planning Hierarchy (Strategic → Operational)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[National Energy Policy] --> L2[State Energy Plan]
    L2 --> L3[Sectoral Energy Strategy Industry Transport Buildings]
    L3 --> L4[Corporate Energy Policy]
    L4 --> L5[Plant Energy Management Plan]
    L5 --> L6[Equipment Operating Procedure]
    L6 --> L7[Operator Level Action]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Explanation:** Each tier **cascades** down. The **National Mission on Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** of India defines goals that translate into PAT scheme obligations, which translate into plant-level SEC targets, which become operator-level daily tasks.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Characterization of Energy Usage (4-Dimensional Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Characterizing Energy Usage] --> B[By Energy Form]
    A --> C[By End Use]
    A --> D[By Time Profile]
    A --> E[By Economic Cost]
    B --> B1[Primary Energy]
    B --> B2[Secondary Energy]
    B --> B3[Useful Energy]
    C --> C1[Motors 60-70 percent]
    C --> C2[Heating 15-20 percent]
    C --> C3[Steam 10-15 percent]
    C --> C4[HVAC Lighting 5-10 percent]
    D --> D1[Base Load]
    D --> D2[Peak Load]
    D --> D3[Load Factor LF]
    E --> E1[Flat Tariff]
    E --> E2[Time of Use ToU]
    E --> E3[Demand Charges kVA]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Strategy Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Select Energy Strategy] --> Q1{Investment Capacity?}
    Q1 -- Limited --> Q2[Quick Wins + Housekeeping]
    Q1 -- Moderate --> Q3[Retrofits + IE4 Motors + VFDs]
    Q1 -- High --> Q4[Cogeneration + Waste Heat Recovery]
    Q1 -- Strategic Long-term --> Q5[Renewables + Process Re-engineering]
    Q2 --> R[Achieve 2-5 percent SEC reduction]
    Q3 --> R2[Achieve 10-25 percent SEC reduction]
    Q4 --> R3[Achieve 15-30 percent SEC reduction]
    Q5 --> R4[Achieve 30-60 percent SEC reduction]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The examiner often asks: *"Enumerate energy strategies from low to high investment."* Always answer in **descending order of capital intensity vs. payback** — housekeeping first, then retrofits, then process redesign, then renewables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Energy Management. List any four of its key objectives.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Energy Management is the systematic planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of energy procurement, conversion, distribution, and utilization to optimize cost, minimize environmental impact, and ensure sustainability.

**Four Key Objectives:**
1. Optimize specific energy consumption (SEC) per unit of output.
2. Reduce energy cost and improve profitability.
3. Minimize greenhouse gas emissions and environmental footprint.
4. Ensure compliance with statutory regulations (Energy Conservation Act 2001, ISO 50001, PAT scheme).

---

### **Q2. What is the Energy Baseline (EnB)? Why is it important in Energy Management?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Energy Baseline (EnB):** The EnB is a quantitative reference point that quantifies the energy performance of an organization, system, process, or facility over a defined historical period, used as the basis for comparison with future performance after energy improvement actions.

**Importance:**
- Provides a **starting reference** for measuring improvements.
- Enables objective computation of **% energy savings**.
- Required by **ISO 50001:2018** as the cornerstone of EnMS.
- Allows valid comparison between **expected vs. actual savings** (M&V).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes that cost students 3–5 marks per answer:
> 1. Writing the **definition of Energy Audit** instead of **Energy Management** (or vice versa).
> 2. Forgetting to include **numerical units** (kWh, TOE, MJ) — losing 1 mark per missing unit.
> 3. Omitting **PDCA cycle** or **ISO 50001 reference** in EnMP answers.
> 4. Writing energy-saving measures without **quantified payback** — examiners want numbers.

---

### **Q3(A). Discuss in detail the Energy Management Program (EnMP) as per ISO 50001 framework. List its essential elements and explain how it integrates with the Plan–Do–Check–Act cycle.** `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Overview of ISO 50001 Energy Management System (EnMS) [7 Marks]

**Definition:** ISO 50001:2018 is the international standard that specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an **Energy Management System (EnMS)**.

**Essential Elements of EnMP under ISO 50001:**

1. **Energy Policy** — Signed declaration by top management committing to continual energy improvement, legal compliance, and resource provision.

2. **Energy Review & Energy Baseline (EnB)** — Initial study to identify current energy consumption patterns, establish EnB, identify Significant Energy Uses (SEUs), and determine Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs).

3. **Objectives, Targets, and Action Plans** — Quantified improvement goals (e.g., 10% reduction in SEC within 2 years) supported by project-level action plans specifying tasks, responsibilities, timelines, budgets, and KPIs.

4. **Operational Controls** — Documented procedures for operating equipment and processes that significantly affect energy use (e.g., boiler tuning, HVAC scheduling, motor maintenance SOPs).

5. **Monitoring, Measurement & Verification (M&V)** — Routine collection and analysis of energy data; calibration of meters; verification of savings using international protocols (e.g., IPMVP – International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol).

6. **Management Review (MRM)** — Top-management-led periodic review of EnMP effectiveness, audit findings, and corrective actions.

#### (b) Integration with PDCA Cycle [7 Marks]

| PDCA Phase | ISO 50001 Activity | Output |
|------------|--------------------|--------|
| **PLAN** | Energy review, baseline, SEUs, O&T, action plans | Energy Policy + EnMP document |
| **DO** | Implement action plans, training, operational controls | Commissioned measures + trained staff |
| **CHECK** | Monitor, measure, audit, verify savings | KPI reports + non-conformities |
| **ACT** | Corrective actions, management review, update EnMP | Revised targets + improved EnMP |

The **PDCA cycle** ensures **continual improvement** — a core ISO 50001 principle. The cycle is repeated annually (or more frequently), with each iteration raising the bar of energy performance.

> **Conclusion:** ISO 50001 provides a globally accepted, structured, and auditable framework for EnMP. Organizations with certified EnMS report **10–30% reduction in energy costs** within 3 years.

---

### **OR**

### **Q3(B). Explain the concept of Characterizing Energy Usage. Discuss the various dimensions and analytical techniques used to characterize energy consumption in an industrial facility.** `[14 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Concept of Characterizing Energy Usage [7 Marks]

Characterizing energy usage means **systematically profiling and quantifying** how energy enters, flows through, and exits a system or facility. It answers four questions:
- **What** forms of energy are used?
- **Where** is energy consumed (end-uses)?
- **When** is energy consumed (time profile)?
- **How much** does it cost?

Without characterization, **energy management is blind** — analogous to budgeting without knowing where money is spent.

#### (b) Dimensions and Analytical Techniques [7 Marks]

**Dimension 1 — By Energy Form:**
- Differentiate **primary (coal, gas, oil)**, **secondary (electricity, steam)**, and **useful energy**.
- Build **Sankey diagrams** to visualize energy losses at each transformation stage.
- Compute **cascade efficiency** from source to end-use.

**Dimension 2 — By End-Use (Sectoral Decomposition):**
- Conduct **end-use metering** of motors, HVAC, lighting, process heat, compressed air.
- Apply **ABC analysis**: 'A' items consume 80% of energy — focus here first.
- Use **pie charts** showing % share of each end-use.

**Dimension 3 — By Time Profile:**
- Generate **daily, weekly, seasonal load curves** (kW vs. hour).
- Compute **Load Factor (LF)** = Average Load ÷ Peak Load.
- Identify **demand peaks** and evaluate **Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff** opportunities.

**Dimension 4 — By Cost:**
- Compute **energy cost per unit** of production (₹/ton, ₹/kWh equivalent).
- Analyze **demand charges (kVA)** vs. **energy charges (kWh)**.
- Benchmark SEC against **industry best practices** (BEE's Sectoral benchmarks).

**Analytical Techniques Used:**
- **Sankey diagrams** — Energy flow visualization.
- **Pareto analysis** — 80/20 prioritization.
- **Trend charts** — Time-series consumption.
- **CUSUM** (Cumulative Sum) — Detecting efficiency drift.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** trending.

> **Conclusion:** Comprehensive characterization is the **foundation for identifying the SEUs** — the high-impact areas where management interventions yield maximum savings.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before entering the exam hall, ensure you can recall all of the following:**

- [x] **Energy Management Definition** — systematic planning, organizing, directing, controlling.
- [x] **Energy Audit Definition** — measurement & verification of energy flows.
- [x] **6 Pillars** — Commitment, Planning, Implementation, M&V, Evaluation, Review.
- [x] **4-E Significance** — Economic, Environmental, Energy Security, Ethical/Regulatory.
- [x] **3 Objectives Levels** — Strategic, Tactical, Operational.
- [x] **4 Dimensions of Characterization** — Energy Form, End-Use, Time Profile, Cost.
- [x] **SEC Formula** — Energy ÷ Production (kWh/ton, MJ/kg).
- [x] **Load Factor Formula** — Average Load ÷ Peak Load.
- [x] **Payback Period** — CAPEX ÷ Annual Monetary Savings.
- [x] **PDCA Cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act (ISO 50001 backbone).
- [x] **EnMP 9 Steps** — Policy → Baseline → Review → SEU → O&T → Action Plan → Implementation → M&V → MRM.
- [x] **Conversion Constants** — 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ; 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 TCE = 7000 kcal/kg.
- [x] **Emission Factor (India)** ≈ 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh.
- [x] **Industrial End-Use Split** — Motors 60-70%, Heating 15-20%, Steam 10-15%, HVAC/Lighting 5-10%.
- [x] **Energy Strategy Hierarchy** — Housekeeping → Retrofits → Process Redesign → Cogeneration → Renewables.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always quantify — never write qualitative statements alone. *"The plant will save energy"* is worth 0 marks; *"The plant will save ₹10.62 lakh/year with a 4.5-month payback"* earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit - Types, Procedure and Techniques, Understanding Energy Costs, Benchmarking, Energy Performance, Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit - Types, Procedure and Techniques, Understanding Energy Costs, Benchmarking, Energy Performance, Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Energy Audit?

An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, documented inspection, survey, and analysis of energy flows in a building, process, or system with the objective of identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs), quantifying energy savings, and recommending measures to optimize energy use without compromising productivity, safety, or comfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (BEE Aligned):** "An energy audit is a systematic procedure undertaken to determine energy consumption, identify energy wastage, and recommend energy-efficient practices in a facility."

> [!NOTE]
> The term **Energy Audit** is the cornerstone of any energy management program. It is the **first step** in the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle of ISO 50001.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Health Checkup Analogy)

Think of an energy audit exactly like a **comprehensive medical health checkup for your factory or building**:

| Medical Checkup | Energy Audit Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Doctor examines patient | Energy Auditor examines facility |
| Blood test / X-Ray | Energy monitoring & measurements |
| Diagnosis of disease | Identification of energy waste |
| Prescribed medicine | Recommended energy-saving measures |
| Follow-up visits | Measurement & Verification (M&V) |

Just as a doctor checks **vitals** (BP, sugar, cholesterol), an energy auditor checks **energy vitals** — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), Energy Performance Index (EPI), load factor, power factor, and demand.

### 1.3 Why is an Energy Audit Essential?

- **Cost Reduction:** 10–30% savings are typically achievable in most facilities.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Aligns with the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** of India and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme.
- **Carbon Footprint Reduction:** Reduces greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
- **Equipment Longevity:** Improves reliability and reduces maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **kWh** — Kilowatt-hour (electrical energy)
> - **TOE** — Tonne of Oil Equivalent (1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh)
> - **MTOE** — Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent
> - **GJ** — Giga Joule (10⁹ J)
> - **kVArh** — Reactive energy unit
> - **kVA** — Apparent power unit (for demand charges)

### 1.4 Quick Snapshot — Types of Energy Audits

> [!NOTE]
> KTU and BEE classify audits into **3 levels**. This is a **direct 3-mark question** in every KTU exam.

**Type 1 — Preliminary Energy Audit (Walk-through Audit):**
- Quick visual inspection, no instruments.
- Identifies obvious wastes and low-cost measures.
- Duration: 1–3 days.
- Cost: Low.

**Type 2 — Detailed Energy Audit:**
- In-depth measurements using instruments (power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, lux meter, infrared thermometer).
- Quantifies energy use by each sub-system.
- Duration: 2–6 weeks.
- Cost: Medium.

**Type 3 — Investment Grade Audit (IGA):**
- Rigorous engineering analysis for project financing.
- Detailed feasibility study, accurate cost-benefit analysis.
- Duration: Several months.
- Cost: High.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Energy Audit Procedure (Step-by-Step Methodology)

The audit follows a **structured 8-step protocol** standardized by BEE and ISO 50002:

**Step 1 — Planning and Organizing:**
- Define audit scope, boundary, objectives, and team composition.
- Collect baseline data: electricity bills, fuel bills, production data for the past 2–3 years.

**Step 2 — Walk-through Inspection:**
- Visual survey of all energy-consuming equipment.
- Interview operators and maintenance staff.
- Identify major energy users (Motors, Boilers, HVAC, Compressors, Lighting).

**Step 3 — Data Collection and Analysis:**
- Gather detailed data on operating hours, load patterns, and efficiency.
- Use historical data and logged trends.

**Step 4 — Detailed Measurements & Monitoring:**
- Use calibrated instruments:
  - Power analyzer → electrical parameters
  - Flue gas analyzer → boiler efficiency
  - Manometer → pressure drop
  - Tachometer → motor speed
  - Pyrometer → temperature
  - Lux meter → illumination

**Step 5 — Identification of Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs):**
- Categorize as **No-Cost (NC)**, **Low-Cost (LC)**, and **Capital-intensive (CI)** measures.
- For each ECO, estimate potential energy savings and implementation cost.

**Step 6 — Economic and Financial Analysis:**
- Calculate payback period, ROI, NPV, IRR.
- Prioritize measures based on financial viability.

**Step 7 — Report Preparation:**
- Executive summary, methodology, findings, recommendations, and implementation roadmap.

**Step 8 — Implementation Support & M&V:**
- Assist in implementation and verify savings using M&V protocols (IPMVP).

### 2.2 Understanding Energy Costs (The Cost Decomposition)

Energy cost is **NOT just the unit rate × consumption.** It is a multi-component structure:

**Component A — Energy Charge (Variable Cost):**
Billed per unit (kWh, m³, litre) of energy consumed.

**Component B — Demand Charge (Fixed Cost per kVA):**
Billed on **maximum kVA** drawn in the billing cycle (typically 15-min or 30-min window). A major hidden cost in commercial and industrial bills.

**Component C — Power Factor Penalty / Incentive:**
- If PF < 0.90, penalty is levied.
- If PF > 0.95, incentive is given.
- PF Penalty = (Target PF - Actual PF) × kVAh × Rate

**Component D — Electricity Duty & Taxes:**
- State electricity duty (varies 5–15%).
- Municipal taxes and cess.

**Component E — Fuel Cost (For Boilers, Furnaces):**
- Cost of coal, diesel, furnace oil, natural gas, biomass.
- Includes transportation and handling charges.

**Component F — Time-of-Use (ToU) Charges:**
- Higher tariff during peak hours (6 PM – 10 PM).
- Lower tariff during off-peak hours (10 PM – 6 AM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Cost Insight:** For industrial consumers, **demand charge + power factor penalty can account for 25–40% of the total electricity bill** — even if you consume ZERO units. This is the most "expensive kWh" in the bill.

### 2.3 Benchmarking

**Benchmarking** is the process of comparing an organization's energy performance against:
- **Internal benchmarks** — own historical performance
- **External benchmarks** — peer industries, sector averages
- **Best-practice benchmarks** — globally recognized efficient plants

**Benchmarking Methods:**
- **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption) ratio** = Actual SEC / Best Practice SEC
- **CUSUM analysis** — cumulative sum of deviations from baseline.
- **Energy Index (EI)** = Plant SEC / Industry Average SEC
- **M&V based benchmarking** using IPMVP protocols.

> [!NOTE]
> An **Energy Index > 1.0** indicates the plant is **less efficient** than the industry average. Target: **Energy Index < 1.0**.

### 2.4 Energy Performance Metrics

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for energy performance:

- **SEC** = Total Energy / Production Output
- **EPI** = Total Energy / Built-up Area (kWh/m²/year) — for buildings
- **Energy Cost per Unit Production**
- **Load Factor** = Average Load / Peak Load
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** for power plants
- **Specific Power Consumption** for compressors, pumps, fans
- **kWh/ton of refrigeration (TR)** for HVAC
- **kg steam/kg fuel** for boilers

### 2.5 Optimum Performance of Existing Facilities

Achieving optimum performance involves 4 layers of optimization:

**Layer 1 — Housekeeping & Operating Practices (No-Cost):**
- Switch off idle equipment
- Maintain proper insulation
- Repair steam/air/water leaks

**Layer 2 — Equipment Maintenance:**
- Cleaning condenser/evaporator coils
- Lubrication, alignment, balancing
- Filter replacement

**Layer 3 — Equipment Tuning & Controls:**
- Reset thermostat setpoints
- Optimize combustion air-fuel ratio
- Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on motors

**Layer 4 — Equipment Replacement:**
- Replace inefficient motors with IE3/IE4 class
- Replace conventional lighting with LED
- Upgrade to energy-efficient chillers and boilers

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{output}}}$ | TOE/ton, kWh/kg | Industry-standard KPI |
| 2 | Energy Performance Index (EPI) | $\text{EPI} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{built-up}}}$ | kWh/m²/year | Used in ECBC / GRIHA buildings |
| 3 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{b} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{steam}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | % | Direct method |
| 4 | Boiler Efficiency (Indirect) | $\eta_{b} = 100 - (\text{Losses})$ | % | Sum of stack, radiation, moisture losses |
| 5 | Cost of Energy Saved (CoES) | $\text{CoES} = E_{\text{saved}} \times R$ | ₹/year | R = unit rate |
| 6 | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Years | Investment / Annual Savings |
| 7 | Return on Investment (ROI) | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{I} \times 100$ | % | Higher is better |
| 8 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{S_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - I$ | ₹ | Discounted cash flow |
| 9 | Power Factor Penalty | $\text{Penalty} = (0.90 - \text{PF}_{\text{actual}}) \times \text{kVAh} \times R$ | ₹ | Only if PF < 0.90 |
| 10 | Total Energy Cost | $\text{TEC} = (kWh \times R_{e}) + (kVA \times R_{d}) + \text{Taxes}$ | ₹/month | e = energy, d = demand |
| 11 | CUSUM | $\text{CUSUM}_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_{i} - E_{\text{baseline}})$ | kWh | Trend monitoring |
| 12 | Plant Load Factor (PLF) | $\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Rated Capacity}} \times 100$ | % | Power plants |
| 13 | Energy Index (Benchmarking) | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{Plant SEC}}{\text{Industry Best SEC}}$ | Ratio | Target < 1.0 |
| 14 | Energy Conversion (kWh to MJ) | $E_{MJ} = kWh \times 3.6$ | MJ | 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ |
| 15 | TOE Conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$ | — | Standard factor |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy audit techniques are used in:
- **Industrial plants** — cement, steel, paper, textiles, chemical, fertilizer
- **Commercial buildings** — malls, hospitals, hotels, IT parks (ECBC compliance)
- **Utilities** — power plants, water treatment, sewage treatment
- **Transport** — fleet management, railway energy efficiency
- **Agriculture** — pump sets, cold storage
- **Renewable integration** — solar rooftop audit, wind farm performance

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem 1: Energy Cost Decomposition (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A manufacturing unit has the following monthly electricity consumption data:
- Energy consumption: **1,50,000 kWh/month**
- Maximum demand recorded: **500 kVA**
- Energy charge rate: **₹ 7.50 per kWh**
- Demand charge rate: **₹ 250 per kVA per month**
- Power factor maintained: **0.92 lagging**
- Electricity duty: **10% of total**
- Monthly production: **10,000 units**

The plant also uses **5,000 litres of diesel/month** at **₹ 80/litre** in a diesel generator for backup.

**Calculate:**
(a) Total electricity bill per month (7 marks)
(b) Total fuel cost per month and Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) per unit production (7 marks)

---

#### (a) Solution — Total Electricity Bill

**Step 1: Calculate Energy Charge (Variable Component)**

$$\text{Energy Charge} = \text{kWh} \times R_{e}$$

$$\text{Energy Charge} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 7.50$$

$$\text{Energy Charge} = \text{₹ } 11{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 2: Calculate Demand Charge (Fixed Component)**

$$\text{Demand Charge} = \text{kVA} \times R_{d}$$

$$\text{Demand Charge} = 500 \times 250$$

$$\text{Demand Charge} = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 3: Calculate Power Factor Penalty**

Since actual PF = 0.92 > 0.90, **no penalty applies.** (PF > 0.90 is acceptable.)

$$\text{PF Penalty} = 0 \text{ (because PF} = 0.92 > 0.90)$$

> [!NOTE]
> If PF had been 0.85, the formula would be:
> $$\text{Penalty} = (0.90 - 0.85) \times \text{kVAh} \times \text{Rate}$$
> $$\text{kVAh} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.85} = 1{,}76{,}471 \text{ kVAh}$$
> $$\text{Penalty} = 0.05 \times 1{,}76{,}471 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 66{,}176$$

**Step 4: Calculate Subtotal before Electricity Duty**

$$\text{Subtotal} = \text{Energy Charge} + \text{Demand Charge} + \text{PF Penalty}$$

$$\text{Subtotal} = 11{,}25{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000 + 0$$

$$\text{Subtotal} = \text{₹ } 12{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5: Add Electricity Duty (10%)**

$$\text{Electricity Duty} = 0.10 \times 12{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 6: Calculate Total Electricity Bill**

$$\text{Total Electricity Bill} = 12{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Electricity Bill} = \text{₹ } 13{,}75{,}000 \text{ per month}}$$

---

#### (b) Solution — Fuel Cost & Specific Energy Consumption

**Step 1: Calculate Diesel Cost (Fuel)**

$$\text{Fuel Cost} = \text{Volume} \times \text{Rate per litre}$$

$$\text{Fuel Cost} = 5{,}000 \times 80$$

$$\text{Fuel Cost} = \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 2: Convert Electrical Energy to MJ**

Using the standard conversion: **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**

$$E_{\text{electric}} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.6 = 5{,}40{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3: Convert Diesel Energy to MJ**

Using the standard conversion: **1 litre diesel ≈ 36 MJ** (calorific value)

$$E_{\text{diesel}} = 5{,}000 \times 36 = 1{,}80{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Energy Consumption in MJ**

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{electric}} + E_{\text{diesel}}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 5{,}40{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 720 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Total Energy Cost**

$$\text{Total Energy Cost} = \text{Electricity Bill} + \text{Fuel Cost}$$

$$\text{Total Energy Cost} = 13{,}75{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Total Energy Cost} = \text{₹ } 17{,}75{,}000 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{production}}}$$

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{7{,}20{,}000 \text{ MJ}}{10{,}000 \text{ units}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SEC} = 72 \text{ MJ/unit} = 0.072 \text{ GJ/unit}}$$

**Step 7: Calculate Energy Cost per Unit**

$$\text{Cost per Unit} = \dfrac{17{,}75{,}000}{10{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Energy Cost per Unit} = \text{₹ } 177.50/\text{unit}}$$

---

### 3.2 Problem 2: Benchmarking & Payback Period (Part-B Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A motor replacement program is proposed. The existing motor (15 kW, 88% efficient) is replaced with a **premium efficiency IE3 motor (91% efficient).** The motor runs **4,000 hours/year** at **75% average load.** Electricity cost is **₹ 8/kWh.** New motor cost is **₹ 45,000** (including installation).

**Calculate:**
(a) Annual energy savings in kWh and ₹ (7 marks)
(b) Simple payback period and ROI (7 marks)

---

#### (a) Solution — Annual Energy Savings

**Step 1: Calculate Output Power (Mechanical)**

$$P_{\text{output}} = P_{\text{rated}} \times \text{Load Factor}$$

$$P_{\text{output}} = 15 \times 0.75 = 11.25 \text{ kW (mechanical)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Input Power for OLD Motor**

$$P_{\text{input,old}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{output}}}{\eta_{\text{old}}}$$

$$P_{\text{input,old}} = \dfrac{11.25}{0.88} = 12.784 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Input Power for NEW Motor**

$$P_{\text{input,new}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{output}}}{\eta_{\text{new}}}$$

$$P_{\text{input,new}} = \dfrac{11.25}{0.91} = 12.363 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Power Saved**

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{input,old}} - P_{\text{input,new}}$$

$$\Delta P = 12.784 - 12.363 = 0.421 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Annual Energy Saved**

$$E_{\text{saved}} = \Delta P \times \text{Operating Hours}$$

$$E_{\text{saved}} = 0.421 \times 4{,}000$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{saved}} = 1{,}684 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Annual Monetary Savings**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = E_{\text{saved}} \times R_{e}$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 1{,}684 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Savings} = \text{₹ } 13{,}472 \text{ per year}}$$

---

#### (b) Solution — Payback Period & ROI

**Step 7: Simple Payback Period (SPP)**

$$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$$

$$\text{SPP} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{13{,}472}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SPP} = 3.34 \text{ years} \approx 3 \text{ years, 4 months}}$$

**Step 8: Return on Investment (ROI)**

$$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{13{,}472}{45{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{ROI} = 29.94\% \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 9: Annual CO₂ Reduction (Bonus — often asked)**

Using emission factor: **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (India grid average)

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ Reduction} = 1{,}684 \times 0.82$$

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ Reduction} = 1{,}381 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} = 1.38 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$

---

### 3.3 Problem 3: Energy Performance Index (EPI) for a Building

**Problem Statement:**
A commercial office building has:
- Total annual electricity consumption: **5,00,000 kWh/year**
- Total annual diesel (DG backup) consumption: **20,000 litres/year**
- Total conditioned area: **2,500 m²**
- Total unconditioned area: **1,500 m²**

**Calculate the Energy Performance Index (EPI).**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert all energy to a common unit (MJ)**

$$E_{\text{electric}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6 = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_{\text{diesel}} = 20{,}000 \times 36 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 = 25{,}20{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 25{,}200 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 2: Calculate EPI in kWh/m²/year (Standard Building Metric)**

$$\text{EPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total kWh}}{\text{Total Built-up Area}}$$

$$\text{EPI} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 + (20{,}000 \times 3.6)}{2{,}500 + 1{,}500}$$

Note: Convert diesel to equivalent kWh using the assumption that **1 kWh ≈ 0.25 litres of diesel** (DG efficiency ~30%, calorific value 36 MJ/litre).

$$\text{kWh}_{\text{diesel}} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 3.6}{1} = 72{,}000 \text{ kWh equivalent}$$

$$\text{EPI} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 + 72{,}000}{4{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EPI} = 143 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) Threshold for Office Buildings:** 100–180 kWh/m²/year depending on climate zone. A value of 143 kWh/m²/year is **moderately compliant** but has improvement potential.
