# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the equivalence ratio (ER) used in gasification and the air-fuel ratio used in combustion. Why is ER maintained below 0.4 in gasifiers?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Combustion** uses an Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) **greater than or equal to 1** to ensure **complete oxidation** of fuel (e.g., AFR for CH₄ ≈ 17.2).
- **Gasification** uses an **Equivalence Ratio (ER) less than 1** (typically 0.25 – 0.40), defined as the ratio of *actual* air supplied to *stoichiometric* air required.
- **Why ER < 0.4?** A low ER deliberately creates an **oxygen-deficient (reducing) atmosphere** that prevents complete combustion of carbon to CO₂. Instead, partial oxidation produces **CO and H₂** — the combustible components of syngas. If ER exceeds 0.4, the system shifts toward **combustion**, lowering syngas calorific value drastically.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion and identify the most sensitive stage in terms of process stability.**

**Model Answer:**
The four stages are:
1. **Hydrolysis** — breakdown of polymers to monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis** — conversion of monomers to volatile fatty acids (VFAs).
3. **Acetogenesis** — conversion of VFAs to acetate, H₂, and CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — production of CH₄ from acetate and H₂/CO₂.

The most sensitive stage is **Methanogenesis**, because methanogenic archaea are:
- Strictly **anaerobic** (intolerant to even trace O₂).
- Slow-growing (**doubling time ~ 5–10 days**).
- Highly **pH-sensitive** (optimum pH 6.8 – 7.4; below 6.5, methane production ceases and VFAs accumulate — a condition called "souring").

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **downdraft biomass gasifier**. List the various zones and the dominant chemical reactions occurring in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier processes **500 kg/hr** of wood chips with moisture content of **12%** (db basis). The LHV of dry wood is **18 MJ/kg**. The syngas produced has a flow rate of **850 Nm³/hr** with an LHV of **5.8 MJ/Nm³**. Calculate the **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A downdraft gasifier is a **fixed-bed, co-current** gasifier where biomass and air flow in the **same downward direction**. It consists of four distinct zones:

**Zone 1 — Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C):**
- Biomass is dried by the hot updraft of flue gases.
- Reaction: $H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$, Endothermic.

**Zone 2 — Pyrolysis Zone (200 – 600 °C):**
- Biomass devolatilizes, releasing condensable vapors, tars, and non-condensable gases.
- The remaining solid is **char**.
- Reaction: $Biomass \rightarrow Char + Tar + Volatiles$

**Zone 3 — Oxidation (Combustion) Zone (700 – 1200 °C):**
- Char and volatiles react with limited O₂.
- Reaction: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ (ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol)
- Reaction: $2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (Partial Oxidation)
- This is the **hottest zone** and supplies heat for endothermic reactions above and below it.

**Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (600 – 900 °C):**
- Hot CO₂ and H₂O are reduced by hot char.
- **Boudouard:** $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (ΔH = +172.5 kJ/mol)
- **Water-Gas:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ (ΔH = +131.4 kJ/mol)
- **Water-Gas Shift:** $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$

The syngas exits at the bottom (along with ash). Downdraft gasifiers produce **relatively low-tar syngas** (~ 0.1 – 0.5 g/Nm³), making them ideal for direct engine coupling.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 500$ kg/hr (wet, with 12% moisture on db basis → mc = 12/112 = 10.71% wet basis)
- LHV of dry wood = 18 MJ/kg
- LHV of wet feed = 18 × (1 − 0.1071) = 16.07 MJ/kg
- Syngas flow = 850 Nm³/hr
- Syngas LHV = 5.8 MJ/Nm³

**Step 1: Energy Input**

$$E_{\text{in}} = 500 \text{ kg/hr} \times 16.07 \text{ MJ/kg} = 8035.71 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 2: Energy Output**

$$E_{\text{out}} = 850 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} \times 5.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 4930 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3: Cold Gas Efficiency**

$$CGE = \dfrac{4930}{8035.71} \times 100 = \boxed{61.35\%}$$

This CGE is **within the typical industrial range of 60 – 75%** for downdraft wood-chip gasifiers. A higher moisture content in the feed would reduce this value, justifying pre-drying as a pre-treatment step.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the **Compressed Biogas (CBG) production** scheme from agricultural waste. What are the **SATAT scheme** targets? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biogas plant produces **2000 Nm³/day** of raw biogas with composition: **CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 1%**. The gas is upgraded to **95% methane purity** and compressed to **200 bar** for use as automotive fuel. Calculate: **(i)** the volume of pure CH₄ obtained per day, **(ii)** the energy content of CBG (LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/Nm³), and **(iii)** the equivalent litres of petrol (LHV of petrol = 32 MJ/L). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**CBG Production Process (from agricultural waste):**

1. **Feedstock Collection & Slurry Preparation:** Agricultural residue (rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, press mud) is mixed with water/cow dung to form a slurry of 8 – 12% Total Solids (TS).
2. **Anaerobic Digester (CSTR / Plug-flow):** Slurry is fed into a sealed digester maintained at **mesophilic (35 – 40 °C)** or **thermophilic (50 – 55 °C)** conditions for **20 – 40 days**. Microbial consortium converts organics to **raw biogas**.
3. **Biogas Upgradation:** Raw biogas is upgraded using:
   - **Water Scrubbing** (PSA / VSA): Dissolves CO₂ and H₂S in water under pressure.
   - **Amine Absorption:** MEA/MDEA solvents selectively absorb CO₂.
   - **Membrane Separation:** Polyimide hollow-fiber membranes separate CO₂.
   The product has **CH₄ purity > 95%**.
4. **Compression:** Multi-stage reciprocating compressor compresses the gas to **200 – 250 bar**.
5. **Storage & Dispensing:** Stored in high-pressure cascades and dispensed to CNG vehicles.

**SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) Scheme (MoPNG, Govt. of India):**
- **Target:** Establish **5000 CBG plants** by **2025 – 2026**.
- **Total CBG production target:** **15 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes)** by 2030.
- Linked to the **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)** transport segment and **Piped Natural Gas (PNG)** domestic supply.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Raw biogas = 2000 Nm³/day
- Composition: CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 1%

**(i) Volume of Pure CH₄:**

$$V_{CH_4} = 2000 \times 0.60 = 1200 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

This is the raw methane. After upgradation to 95% purity, the methane recovered depends on recovery efficiency. Assuming **95% methane recovery** (typical for PSA):

$$V_{CH_4,\text{upgraded}} = 1200 \times 0.95 = 1140 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day of pure CH}_4$$

**(ii) Energy Content of CBG:**

$$E_{CBG} = 1140 \text{ Nm}^3 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 40,812 \text{ MJ/day}$$

$$E_{CBG} = \dfrac{40,812}{3600} = 11.337 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Petrol Volume:**

$$V_{\text{petrol}} = \dfrac{40,812 \text{ MJ}}{32 \text{ MJ/L}} = \boxed{1275.4 \text{ litres of petrol per day}}$$

This daily output, when scaled annually, can displace approximately **4.65 lakh litres/year** of petrol — a meaningful contribution to India's import substitution goal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LHV and HHV of methane** — LHV = 50 MJ/kg (35.8 MJ/Nm³), HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg (39.8 MJ/Nm³). For *engine calculations*, always use LHV.
> 2. **Moisture content basis confusion** — "12% moisture" can be on a *dry basis (db)* or *wet basis (wb)*. Always re-confirm. (Dry basis: 12% db = 10.71% wb; Wet basis: 12% wb = 13.64% db).
> 3. **Methanogenesis pH cliff** — Students often write pH "between 5 and 8" for AD. The correct range is **6.8 – 7.4**; below 6.5, the digester sours.
> 4. **Stoichiometric ethanol yield** — Many students forget to convert from *moles to mass* and answer "2 moles" instead of "92 g per 180 g glucose".
> 5. **Equivalence Ratio inversion** — Some students define ER inversely (1/ER). The KTU convention is **ER = Actual/Stoichiometric**, with ER < 1 for gasification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gasification** is a thermochemical, partial-oxidation process producing **syngas**; ER is held between **0.25 – 0.40**.
- [x] **Pyrolysis** is purely thermal decomposition in the **absence of oxygen**; "hot & fast" → bio-oil; "cool & slow" → char.
- [x] **Fermentation** uses **yeast** to convert sugars to ethanol with a **theoretical yield of 51.1%** by mass.
- [x] **Anaerobic Digestion** is a **four-stage microbial process** ending in **methanogenesis** — the most pH-sensitive stage.
- [x] **CBG** is upgraded biogas compressed to **200 bar** with **≥ 95% CH₄ purity** under India's **SATAT scheme**.
- [x] **LHV of methane = 35.8 MJ/Nm³** is a high-yield constant for KTU numericals.
- [x] **Cold Gas Efficiency (60 – 75%)** is the standard performance metric for biomass gasifiers.
- [x] **Syngas from air gasification** has low calorific value (**4 – 7 MJ/Nm³**) due to N₂ dilution; **O₂/steam gasification** yields higher CV (**10 – 20 MJ/Nm³**).
- [x] **Bio-methanation** is sensitive to **temperature, pH, C/N ratio (optimum 20 – 30), and hydraulic retention time (HRT 20 – 40 days)**.
- [x] **Compressed Biogas** serves as a direct substitute for **CNG** and is fully interchangeable with natural gas at the dispensing nozzle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation Opportunities - Electrical ECOs, Thermodynamic ECOs in Chemical Process Industry, ECOs in Residential and Commercial Buildings, Energy Conservation Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation Opportunities - Electrical ECOs, Thermodynamic ECOs in Chemical Process Industry, ECOs in Residential and Commercial Buildings, Energy Conservation Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO)** is any technically feasible, economically justifiable, and operationally implementable modification in a process, equipment, system, or building that results in a measurable reduction in energy consumption (in kWh, MJ, GJ, or TOE) without compromising the quality, quantity, or functional output of the product or service. ECOs are identified systematically during an **Energy Audit** (Type 1, Type 2, or Type 3) and are evaluated using financial metrics such as **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **Return on Investment (ROI)** before implementation.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking Bucket" Model

Imagine you are a farmer carrying water in a bucket with several small holes punched into its sides. Your job is to water the field (your output / production). 

- The **water poured in** represents the **energy input** (electricity, fuel, steam) supplied to your industry or building.
- The **leaks** represent **inefficiencies** — wasted energy leaving the system as heat, vibration, friction, poor insulation, reactive power, or exhaust losses.
- **Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)** are the actions you take to either **(a) plug the leaks** (efficiency improvement) or **(b) carry a smaller, better-designed bucket** (load reduction / demand-side management).

If you just pour more water in to compensate for the leaks, your operating cost rises every month, but your yield stays the same. The smarter approach is to **plug the leaks** — and an energy auditor's job is to identify every single leak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:**
> ECOs are **NOT the same as Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** in strict terminology:
> - **ECO** = The *identified opportunity* (the diagnosis).
> - **ECM** = The *implemented measure* (the cure).
> In exam answers, clearly distinguish: *"An ECO was identified in the compressed air line; the ECM adopted was installation of a VSD-driven compressor, reducing specific power consumption from 0.18 to 0.12 kWh/m³."*

### 1.2 Classification of ECOs — KTU Module 4 Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus for **OECHT614 Module 4** groups ECOs into four major families:

| ECO Family | Subsystem Focus | Typical Energy Saving Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical ECOs** | Motors, transformers, lighting, cables, capacitors | **5 % – 30 %** of electrical bill |
| **Thermodynamic ECOs in Chemical Process Industry** | Boilers, furnaces, heat exchangers, distillation columns, reactors, waste-heat recovery | **10 % – 40 %** of thermal bill |
| **ECOs in Residential & Commercial Buildings** | HVAC, envelope, lighting, BEE star appliances, building management | **20 % – 50 %** of building energy |
| **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** — Implementation Layer | Payback economics, M&V protocol, ESCO contracting | Cross-cutting |

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Standard electricity tariff in India (industrial, average): **₹ 7 – ₹ 9 per kWh** (varies by state and HT/LT category).
- Standard thermal energy cost: **₹ 1.5 – ₹ 3.5 per kg of steam** (depending on fuel).
- Standard furnace oil calorific value: **GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg** (≈ 42.7 MJ/kg).
- Standard coal GCV (Indian grade): **3,500 – 5,500 kcal/kg**.
- 1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = **42 GJ = 11,630 kWh** (thermal).
- Standard payback acceptance threshold in Indian industries: **SPP ≤ 2 years** for "no-brainer" projects; **SPP ≤ 4–5 years** for capital-intensive retrofits.
- Standard insulation surface temperature target: **≤ 50 °C** at ambient 30 °C.
- Standard power factor correction target: **PF ≥ 0.95 lagging**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always quote these constants with their **units** and **source**. If the question states a particular fuel, use *its* GCV, not a generic value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrical Energy Conservation Opportunities (Electrical ECOs)

Electrical energy in most Indian industries accounts for **20 % – 60 %** of the total energy bill. The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus focuses on the following electrical ECO families:

### 2.1.1 Motor Systems (Largest Single Electrical Load)

Motors consume **~ 65 – 70 %** of all industrial electricity globally. ECOs in motor systems are therefore the highest-impact opportunities in the entire electrical ECO family.

**Step-by-Step Logic of Motor ECO Identification:**

1. **Step 1 — Inventory & Loading Analysis:** Identify every motor ≥ 5 kW. Measure its **loading factor** using a clamp-on power analyzer.
2. **Step 2 — Compare to Efficiency Curve:** Motors operate at peak efficiency only between **60 % – 100 %** load. Below 50 % loading, efficiency drops sharply (the "low-load penalty").
3. **Step 3 — Identify ECOs:**
   - Replace **standard efficiency (IE1/IE2)** motors with **IE3 / IE4 / IE5 (premium efficiency)** motors.
   - Right-size oversized motors (a 30 kW motor driving a 12 kW load wastes energy and incurs poor PF penalty).
   - Install **Variable Speed Drives (VSDs / VFDs)** on centrifugal loads (pumps, fans, compressors) where flow varies.
   - Use **Soft Starters** to reduce starting current and torque shock.
   - Use **Direct-On-Line (DOL)** replacement for star-delta where starting transients are short.
4. **Step 4 — Quantify Savings:** Annual kWh saved × tariff = ₹ saved/year.
5. **Step 5 — Financial Test:** Apply payback formula (see Section 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Loads (Fan / Pump / Compressor):**
> - Flow $\propto$ Speed (Q₁/Q₂ = N₁/N₂)
> - Pressure / Head $\propto$ Speed² (H₁/H₂ = (N₁/N₂)²)
> - Power $\propto$ Speed³ (P₁/P₂ = (N₁/N₂)³)
> 
> **This is the single most tested concept in Electrical ECOs.** Reducing fan speed by just 20 % reduces power consumption by **≈ 49 %**, not 20 %.

### 2.1.2 Transformer ECOs

- Replace **oil-filled distribution transformers** with **amorphous metal core transformers** (loss reduction 60 – 70 %).
- Operate transformers at **40 – 80 % loading** (peak efficiency zone).
- Maintain **Total Losses** = No-load loss + Load loss.
- Use **on-load tap changers (OLTC)** to maintain voltage stability.
- Parallel transformers intelligently — shut down one unit during low-load periods (N-1 redundancy logic).

### 2.1.3 Power Factor Correction (PFC) ECO

Low power factor (PF) means higher line current for the same useful power → higher I²R losses → penalty from electricity board.

**Capacitor kVAr required:**

$$Q_c = P \times (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$$

where P is the active power in kW, φ₁ is the original angle, and φ₂ is the corrected target angle.

### 2.1.4 Lighting ECOs

- Replace **incandescent / halogen / FTL (T12/T8)** with **LED** (saving 50 – 75 %).
- Install **occupancy sensors**, **daylight dimming controls**, and **task lighting zoning**.
- Use **light-coloured walls and ceilings** to improve lumen utilization.
- Maintain **lux levels as per IS 3646** (not over-illumination).

### 2.1.5 Cable and Distribution ECOs

- Upsize undersized cables to reduce I²R losses.
- Improve **power factor** to reduce line current.
- Re-balance three-phase loads (reduce neutral current).
- Install **harmonic filters** if non-linear loads (VSDs, UPS, SMPS) cause distortion (THD > 5 %).

### 2.1.6 Compressed Air System ECOs

Often called the **"most expensive utility"** because only **~ 10 %** of input energy reaches the point of use; the rest is lost as heat in compression.

ECOs: leak repair (typically 20 – 30 % savings), reduce operating pressure by 1 bar (saves ~ 7 % energy), use VSD compressors, recover compressor heat for water heating.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic ECOs in the Chemical Process Industry

The chemical industry is **energy-intensive by nature** — typical specific energy consumption in a refinery is **6 – 8 GJ per tonne of crude processed**. Thermodynamic ECOs focus on **exergy destruction reduction** (i.e., minimizing irreversibility).

### 2.2.1 Boiler & Steam System ECOs

A typical **shell-and-tube fire-tube boiler** with **steam generation D kg/h** and **fuel consumption F kg/h** has efficiency:

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{D (h_g - h_{fw})}{GCV \times F} \times 100$$

where $h_g$ is enthalpy of saturated steam and $h_{fw}$ is enthalpy of feed water.

**Stack-gas loss** (typically the largest single loss, 8 – 25 %):

$$L_{stack} = \frac{m_g \, C_p \, (T_g - T_a)}{GCV \times F} \times 100$$

**ECOs:**
- Reduce excess air from 30 % to 15 % (saves 1 – 3 %).
- Install **economizers** to preheat feed water using flue gas (recovers 4 – 6 %).
- Install **air preheaters** (recovers 2 – 4 %).
- Insulate steam lines, valves, flanges.
- Recover **flash steam** from condensate.
- Use **blowdown heat recovery** (BD heat exchanger).
- Install **steam traps** with proper maintenance; a leaking steam trap can lose thousands of ₹/month.
- Replace **fire-tube boilers** with **water-tube boilers** (better efficiency, faster steaming).

### 2.2.2 Furnace ECOs

- Use **ceramic fibre / refractory** lining with low thermal conductivity.
- Preheat combustion air using waste heat.
- Use **oxygen-enriched combustion** (3 – 5 % O₂ increase → 5 – 10 % fuel saving).
- Optimize **air-fuel ratio** via O₂ trim control.
- Insulate doors, peepholes, and charging openings.

### 2.2.3 Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) ECOs — Pinch Analysis

The most rigorous thermodynamic ECO tool. The **Pinch Point** defines the **Minimum Heating Utility (Qh,min)** and **Minimum Cooling Utility (Qc,min)**:

$$Q_{h,min} = \sum_{i \in Hot} ( \Delta H_i ) - \sum_{j \in Cold} ( \Delta H_j ) \quad \text{(when hot streams dominate above pinch)}$$

**ECOs derived from Pinch Analysis:**
- Add **process-process heat exchangers** to recover heat that would otherwise go to the utility.
- Modify process streams (ΔT, flow) to shift the pinch and reduce utility demand.
- Re-pipe streams, install **Plate Heat Exchangers (PHE)**, **Spiral Heat Exchangers**, or **Compact Heat Exchangers**.

### 2.2.4 Distillation Column ECOs

Distillation is the **largest energy consumer** in chemical/refining — accounts for **~ 40 %** of all process energy.

**ECOs:**
- Improve **reflux ratio** (operate closer to minimum reflux, but watch product purity).
- Install **side condensers / side reboilers** (inter-stage heat removal reduces irreversibility).
- Use **heat pumping** (vapour recompression — mechanical or thermal).
- Use **dividing-wall columns** (DWC) — saves up to 30 % energy for three-product separations.
- Use **structured packing** instead of trays (lower ΔP, better mass transfer).
- Operate at **lower pressure** (if possible) to reduce reboiler duty.

### 2.2.5 Reactor ECOs

- Operate at **optimal conversion** (100 % conversion is rarely optimal economically).
- Use **better catalyst** to lower operating temperature/pressure.
- Use **membrane reactors** for in-situ product separation.
- Use **microreactors** for exothermic reactions (better heat removal).

### 2.2.6 Waste-Heat Recovery (WHR) ECOs

| Source Stream | Typical Temperature | Recovery Technology |
|---|---|---|
| Flue gas (boiler) | 200 – 350 °C | Economizer, air preheater |
| Flue gas (furnace) | 400 – 900 °C | Waste Heat Boiler (WHB), ORC |
| Hot process streams | 100 – 300 °C | PHE, Shell-and-tube HEX |
| Condensate | 80 – 95 °C | Flash steam, hot water |
| Hot products | 100 – 250 °C | Run-around coil, thermal wheel |
| Compressor inter-stage cooling | 80 – 150 °C | Heat to boiler feed water |

**Advanced WHR:** **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** for low-grade (80 – 300 °C) heat; **Kalina Cycle** using ammonia-water mixture for even lower temperatures.

---

## 2.3 ECOs in Residential and Commercial Buildings

Buildings in India consume **~ 33 %** of total electricity and are the second-largest GHG emitter after power generation. The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** and **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** govern this sector.

### 2.3.1 Building Envelope ECOs

- **Roof insulation** with **reflective foil + insulation** (reduces heat gain by 30 – 50 %).
- **Wall insulation** (cavity wall, AAC blocks, double-glazed windows).
- **Window-to-wall ratio (WWR) ≤ 40 %** (ECBC).
- **Low-E double-glazed glass** (U-value ≤ 2.0 W/m²K).
- **External shading** (chajjas, louvers, fins).
- **Cool roof coatings** (SRI ≥ 78).

### 2.3.2 HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning) ECOs

HVAC accounts for **~ 50 %** of building electricity. Major ECOs:

- **Chiller plant optimization:** COP improvement from 3.0 to 5.0 saves 40 %.
- **Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF)** systems for part-load efficiency.
- **Chilled water temperature reset** based on outdoor air.
- **Condenser water reset** to take advantage of cold ambient air.
- **Economizer cycle:** use outside air when T_out < T_setpoint.
- **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** — ice storage for peak shaving.
- **Variable Air Volume (VAV)** systems.
- **Free cooling** during winter months.
- Set thermostat to **24 °C (cooling) / 26 °C (heating)** — every 1 °C increase saves ~ 6 % energy.
- **Regular filter cleaning, coil cleaning, refrigerant top-up.**

### 2.3.3 Lighting ECOs (Buildings)

Already covered above; building-specific additions:

- **Daylight harvesting** with photosensors.
- **Occupancy sensors** in conference rooms, washrooms, corridors.
- **LED + smart controls** (Zigbee / KNX / DALI).
- **Task lighting zoning.**

### 2.3.4 BEE Star Labelled Appliances

Replace ordinary appliances with **5-star BEE-rated** products:

| Appliance | 5-Star Savings (vs 1-star) |
|---|---|
| Air Conditioner (1.5 ton) | ~ 35 % electricity |
| Refrigerator (300 L) | ~ 40 % |
| LED TV | ~ 30 % |
| Ceiling Fan | ~ 50 % |
| Geyser | ~ 20 % |
| LED Bulb | ~ 80 % vs incandescent |

### 2.3.5 Renewable Integration ECOs

- **Rooftop solar PV** (saves 50 – 90 % of daytime grid bill).
- **Solar water heaters** (saves 100 % of water heating energy in homes).
- **Solar passive architecture** — orientation, ventilation, thermal mass.

### 2.3.6 Building Management System (BMS) ECO

A **BMS / BEMS** monitors and controls HVAC, lighting, fire, security. Savings: **10 – 25 %** of building energy.

---

## 2.4 Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) — Implementation Layer

Once ECOs are identified, they must be converted into **ECMs** with technical and financial validation.

### 2.4.1 Categories of ECMs

1. **Low-cost / No-cost measures:** housekeeping, leak repair, insulation topping up, setpoint reset.
2. **Medium-cost measures:** capacitor banks, VSD on motors, LED retrofits.
3. **High-cost measures:** chiller replacement, boiler replacement, process redesign.

### 2.4.2 Implementation Pathway (KTU framework)

**Identify (Energy Audit) → Evaluate (Technical + Economic) → Approve → Implement → Measure & Verify (M&V).**

### 2.4.3 M&V Protocol (IPMVP)

The **International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol (IPMVP)** defines four options (A, B, C, D) to verify actual savings post-implementation.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor Loading Factor | $LF = \dfrac{P_{measured}}{P_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Identify oversized motors |
| 2 | Affinity Law — Flow | $Q_1/Q_2 = N_1/N_2$ | — | Fan, pump, compressor |
| 3 | Affinity Law — Power | $P_1/P_2 = (N_1/N_2)^3$ | — | VSD energy saving |
| 4 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{D(h_g - h_{fw})}{GCV \cdot F} \times 100$ | % | Thermal audit |
| 5 | Stack Loss | $L = \dfrac{m_g C_p (T_g - T_a)}{GCV \cdot F} \times 100$ | % | Boiler tuning |
| 6 | PFC Capacitor | $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | kVAr | PF improvement |
| 7 | Chiller COP | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | — | HVAC efficiency |
| 8 | U-value of Wall | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum (L_i/k_i) + R_{se}}$ | W/m²K | Building envelope |
| 9 | Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{capex} - Incentives}{A_{annual\,saving}}$ | Years | Economic test |
| 10 | NPV | $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | ₹ | Long-term test |
| 11 | IRR | $NPV = 0$ at $r = IRR$ | % | Compared to MARR |
| 12 | LCC | $LCC = C_{capex} + \sum \dfrac{C_{opex}}{(1+r)^t} - \dfrac{S_v}{(1+r)^n}$ | ₹ | Lifecycle costing |
| 13 | Payback w/ discount | $DPP = \dfrac{\ln(C_0/A)}{\ln(1+r)}$ | Years | Discounted payback |
| 14 | Annual Energy Saved | $\Delta E = E_{before} - E_{after}$ | kWh/year | M&V verification |
| 15 | Carbon Reduction | $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}$ | kgCO₂/year | Sustainability KPI |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Savings from VSD on a Centrifugal Pump

**Problem Statement:** A centrifugal pump operates at full speed (N₂ = 100 %) for 24 hours/day. The actual flow demand is only 80 % of design flow. Find the **energy savings percentage** when a VSD is installed.

**Step 1 — Establish Affinity Law (Power):**

$$P_1 / P_2 = (N_1 / N_2)^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Affinity Law (Flow):**

$$Q_1 / Q_2 = N_1 / N_2$$

Since the desired flow is 80 % of design, we set:

$$Q_1 / Q_2 = 0.80 \implies N_1 / N_2 = 0.80$$

**Step 3 — Substitute in Power Affinity Law:**

$$P_1 / P_2 = (0.80)^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cube:**

$$(0.80)^3 = 0.80 \times 0.80 \times 0.80 = 0.64 \times 0.80 = 0.512$$

**Step 5 — State the result:**

$$\boxed{P_1 / P_2 = 0.512}$$

**Step 6 — Compute percentage savings:**

$$\text{Savings \%} = (1 - 0.512) \times 100 = 48.8\%$$

**Conclusion:** Reducing pump speed by just 20 % reduces power consumption by **48.8 %**. This is why **VSD retrofit on centrifugal loads** is the single most cost-effective ECO in any industry. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Payback Period for a Boiler Insulation ECM

**Given Data (Sample Examination Problem):**
- Old boiler stack temperature: $T_g = 320 \, °C$
- Ambient temperature: $T_a = 30 \, °C$
- Flue gas mass flow rate: $m_g = 2200 \, \text{kg/h}$
- Specific heat of flue gas: $C_p = 1.05 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- Fuel (furnace oil) GCV: $10{,}200 \, \text{kcal/kg}$ = $42{,}700 \, \text{kJ/kg}$
- Fuel consumption: $F = 200 \, \text{kg/h}$
- Operating hours: 300 days/year × 16 hours/day = **4800 h/year**
- After insulation: $T_g$ drops to $200 \, °C$
- Cost of insulation: ₹ 4,50,000
- Fuel cost: ₹ 65/kg

**Step 1 — Stack Loss before insulation:**

$$L_1 = \frac{m_g C_p (T_{g1} - T_a)}{GCV \cdot F} \times 100$$

$$L_1 = \frac{2200 \times 1.05 \times (320 - 30)}{42{,}700 \times 200} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$2200 \times 1.05 = 2310$$

$$2310 \times 290 = 669{,}900 \, \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$42{,}700 \times 200 = 8{,}540{,}000 \, \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Stack loss percentage:**

$$L_1 = \frac{669{,}900}{8{,}540{,}000} \times 100 = 7.844\%$$

**Step 5 — Stack Loss after insulation ($T_{g2} = 200 °C$):**

$$L_2 = \frac{2200 \times 1.05 \times (200 - 30)}{42{,}700 \times 200} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{2310 \times 170}{8{,}540{,}000} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{392{,}700}{8{,}540{,}000} \times 100 = 4.598\%$$

**Step 6 — Heat saved per hour:**

$$\Delta L = L_1 - L_2 = 7.844 - 4.598 = 3.246\%$$

$$\Delta Q_{saved} = 0.03246 \times GCV \times F = 0.03246 \times 42{,}700 \times 200$$

$$= 277{,}200 \, \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 7 — Fuel saved per hour:**

$$F_{saved} = \frac{\Delta Q_{saved}}{GCV} = \frac{277{,}200}{42{,}700} = 6.49 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 8 — Annual fuel saving:**

$$F_{annual} = 6.49 \times 4800 = 31{,}160 \, \text{kg/year}$$

**Step 9 — Monetary saving per year:**

$$\text{₹ saved} = 31{,}160 \times 65 = ₹ \, 20{,}25{,}400 \approx ₹ 20.25 \, \text{lakh/year}$$

**Step 10 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \frac{\text{Capex}}{\text{Annual saving}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{20{,}25{,}400} = 0.222 \, \text{years}$$

$$SPP \approx 2.67 \, \text{months}$$

**Conclusion:** Insulation ECM is a **no-brainer** — payback < 6 months. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Factor Correction Capacitor Sizing

**Given:** A factory draws 800 kW at PF = 0.75 lag. The electricity board requires PF = 0.95 lag. Compute the kVAr rating of the capacitor bank to be installed.

**Step 1 — Compute original reactive power (Q₁):**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41°$$

$$\tan \phi_1 = \tan(41.41°) = 0.8819$$

$$Q_1 = P \times \tan \phi_1 = 800 \times 0.8819 = 705.5 \, \text{kVAr}$$

**Step 2 — Compute target reactive power (Q₂):**

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$$

$$\tan \phi_2 = \tan(18.19°) = 0.3287$$

$$Q_2 = 800 \times 0.3287 = 263.0 \, \text{kVAr}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor rating:**

$$Q_c = Q_1 - Q_2 = 705.5 - 263.0 = 442.5 \, \text{kVAr}$$

**Step 4 — Line current reduction:**

$$I_1 = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot PF_1} = \frac{800{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.75} = 1483 \, \text{A}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{800{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.95} = 1171 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Current reduction percentage:**

$$\Delta I\% = \frac{1483 - 1171}{1483} \times 100 = 21.0\%$$

**Conclusion:** A **442.5 kVAr capacitor bank** improves PF from 0.75 → 0.95, reducing line current by 21 % and eliminating PF penalty. ✅

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined Wall U-Value for Building Envelope

**Given Wall Composition (outdoor → indoor):**
- 12 mm cement plaster: $k = 0.72 \, \text{W/m·K}$, $L = 0.012 \, \text{m}$
- 230 mm brick: $k = 0.81 \, \text{W/m·K}$, $L = 0.230 \, \text{m}$
- 12 mm gypsum plaster: $k = 0.42 \, \text{W/m·K}$, $L = 0.012 \, \text{m}$

Surface resistances: $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{se} = 0.04 \, \text{m²K/W}$

**Step 1 — Compute individual layer resistances:**

$$R_1 = L_1/k_1 = 0.012/0.72 = 0.0167$$

$$R_2 = L_2/k_2 = 0.230/0.81 = 0.2840$$

$$R_3 = L_3/k_3 = 0.012/0.42 = 0.0286$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{se}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.0167 + 0.2840 + 0.0286 + 0.04 = 0.4993 \, \text{m²K/W}$$

**Step 3 — U-value:**

$$U = 1 / R_{total} = 1 / 0.4993 = 2.003 \, \text{W/m²K}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to ECBC limit (wall U ≤ 2.0 W/m²K for composite climate):**

The wall is **borderline-compliant**; a thin layer of insulation (e.g., 25 mm XPS with k = 0.028 W/m·K, contributing R = 0.025/0.028 = 0.893) would reduce U to:

$$U_{new} = 1 / (0.4993 + 0.893) = 1 / 1.392 = 0.718 \, \text{W/m²K}$$

— a **64 % reduction** in heat gain through the wall. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Decision Table for ECO Selection

| Industry / Building Type | Highest-Impact ECO | Estimated Saving | Typical SPP (years) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cement plant | Preheater / Pre-calciner waste heat recovery | 8 – 12 % | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Steel plant (reheating furnace) | Regenerative burner + recuperator | 15 – 25 % | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| Textile (spinning) | Motor + VSD + steam leak repair | 10 – 20 % | 0.8 – 2.0 |
| Fertilizer (ammonia) | Process integration / Pinch | 15 – 30 % | 2.0 – 4.0 |
| Pulp & paper | Lime kiln heat recovery | 12 – 18 % | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Refinery | Distillation column heat pumping | 20 – 30 % | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Hotel / Office building | Chiller + lighting retrofit | 25 – 40 % | 1.5 – 3.5 |
| Hospital | BMS + steam sterilization optimization | 15 – 25 % | 2.0 – 4.0 |
| Data centre | Free cooling + hot/cold aisle containment | 30 – 50 % | 2.5 – 5.0 |
| Residential | 5-star appliances + rooftop solar | 40 – 70 % | 3.0 – 7.0 |

---

## 3.6 NPV-Based Economic Decision (Worked Example)

**Given:**
- Capex for VSD on a 30 kW motor: ₹ 2,50,000
- Annual energy saving: 28,000 kWh
- Tariff: ₹ 8/kWh (with 5 % annual escalation)
- Project life: 10 years
- Discount rate (MARR): 12 %

**Step 1 — Year 1 saving:** $A_1 = 28{,}000 \times 8 = ₹ \, 2{,}24{,}000$

**Step 2 — With 5 % escalation, Year t saving:** $A_t = 2{,}24{,}000 \times (1.05)^{t-1}$

**Step 3 — Discount each year at 12 %:** Present Value = $A_t / (1.12)^t$

**Step 4 — Total PV of savings (geometric series with combined growth rate g = 5 %, discount r = 12 %):**

$$PV = A_1 \times \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+r}\right)^n}{r - g}$$

$$PV = 2{,}24{,}000 \times \frac{1 - (1.05/1.12)^{10}}{0.12 - 0.05}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the term (1.05/1.12) = 0.9375:**

$$(0.9375)^{10} = 0.5129$$

**Step 6 — Numerator:** $1 - 0.5129 = 0.4871$

**Step 7 — Denominator:** $0.12 - 0.05 = 0.07$

**Step 8 — Ratio:** $0.4871 / 0.07 = 6.959$

**Step 9 — PV of total savings:**

$$PV = 2{,}24{,}000 \times 6.959 = ₹ \, 15{,}58{,}900$$

**Step 10 — NPV:**

$$NPV = PV - Capex = 15{,}58{,}900 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹ \, 13{,}08{,}900$$

**Conclusion:** Strongly positive NPV → VSD ECM is **economically justified** at 12 % MARR. ✅

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define "Energy Conservation Opportunity" (ECO). How is it different from an "Energy Conservation Measure" (ECM)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Energy Conservation Opportunity (ECO)** is an identified potential action that, when implemented, can reduce energy consumption in a process or system without affecting output quality. An **Energy Conservation Measure (ECM)** is the actual implemented action — i.e., an ECO that has been approved and executed. The difference is that an ECO is a **diagnosis** (e.g., "replace old motor with IE3 motor"), whereas an ECM is the **cure** (e.g., "Replaced 7.5 kW standard motor with IE3 motor; verified saving = 1,250 kWh/year").

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the "Affinity Law" and its importance in variable-speed-drive (VSD) energy savings for centrifugal pumps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The affinity law for centrifugal machines states that **power is proportional to the cube of speed**. If flow is reduced to 80 % by reducing speed, the input power drops to (0.8)³ = 0.512, i.e., a **48.8 %** reduction in power. Hence installing a VSD on a centrifugal pump or fan can save up to 50 % electrical energy even with a modest 20 % flow reduction. This law justifies the VSD as the most cost-effective ECM in fluid-handling systems.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain, with examples, **any five Electrical Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs)** applicable in a typical Indian industry. State the typical energy savings range and payback period for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **centrifugal pump** delivers 200 m³/h at 50 m head when running at 1450 rpm. A VSD is installed and the speed is reduced to **1160 rpm**. Compute the **(i)** new flow, **(ii)** new head, and **(iii)** percentage power saving, assuming pump efficiency remains constant. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### (a) Five Electrical ECOs (7 marks)

1. **Premium-Efficiency Motor Replacement:** Replace IE1/IE2 motors with IE3/IE4. Typical saving: **2 – 5 %** per motor, SPP **2 – 4 years**.

2. **Variable Speed Drives on Centrifugal Loads:** VSDs on pumps/fans/compressors save **30 – 60 %** energy when flow varies. SPP **0.8 – 2.0 years**.

3. **Power Factor Correction:** Installing capacitor banks to raise PF from 0.80 to 0.95 saves **~ 5 %** of the electricity bill by reducing PF penalty and I²R losses. SPP **0.5 – 1.5 years**.

4. **LED Lighting Retrofit:** Replacing FTL/CFL with LED reduces lighting energy by **50 – 75 %**. SPP **1.0 – 2.5 years**.

5. **Compressed Air Leak Repair & Pressure Optimization:** A typical plant has **20 – 30 %** air leakage. Repairing leaks and reducing pressure by 1 bar saves **~ 7 – 10 %** compressor energy. SPP **< 1 year**.

6. *(Bonus — write any one of the following if you have space)*
   - **Harmonic filtering** with active/passive filters to reduce THD losses.
   - **Transformer loss reduction** with amorphous metal cores.
   - **Cable re-sizing** to reduce I²R losses in long feeders.

#### (b) Pump Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:** $Q_1 = 200 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $H_1 = 50 \, \text{m}$, $N_1 = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$, $N_2 = 1160 \, \text{rpm}$.

**Step 1 — Speed ratio:**

$$N_1 / N_2 = 1450 / 1160 = 1.25$$

$$\text{Equivalently, } N_2 / N_1 = 0.80$$

**Step 2 — New flow (Affinity Law 1):**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times (N_2 / N_1) = 200 \times 0.80 = 160 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — New head (Affinity Law 2):**

$$H_2 = H_1 \times (N_2 / N_1)^2 = 50 \times (0.80)^2 = 50 \times 0.64 = 32 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Power ratio (Affinity Law 3):**

$$P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3 = (0.80)^3 = 0.512$$

**Step 5 — Percentage power saving:**

$$\Delta P \% = (1 - 0.512) \times 100 = 48.8\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) New flow** = **160 m³/h**
- **(ii) New head** = **32 m**
- **(iii) Power saving** = **48.8 %**

> ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs) in Residential and Commercial Buildings**. Cover building envelope, HVAC, lighting, and BEE star labelling. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 250 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) chiller operates for 3000 hours/year with an average **COP of 2.8**. The chiller is replaced with a new chiller having **COP of 4.5**. Compute the **annual electrical energy saved** and the **annual monetary saving** at ₹ 8/kWh. If the new chiller costs ₹ 35 lakh, compute the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### (a) Building ECOs (7 marks)

**Building Envelope ECOs:**
- Roof insulation (reflective foil + insulation) → 30 – 50 % reduction in heat gain through roof.
- Wall insulation (cavity wall, AAC blocks) → 15 – 25 % reduction.
- Low-E double-glazed windows → 30 % reduction in solar heat gain.
- Cool roof coating with **SRI ≥ 78** → roof surface temperature drop of 10 – 15 °C.
- Maintain **Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR) ≤ 40 %** as per ECBC.

**HVAC ECOs:**
- Chiller replacement (COP 2.8 → 4.5+).
- Chilled water temperature reset based on outdoor air.
- Free cooling economizer cycle.
- Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) for part-load efficiency.
- VAV (Variable Air Volume) air handling.
- Thermal Energy Storage (TES) — ice storage for peak demand shaving.
- Set thermostat to **24 °C in cooling, 26 °C in heating**.
- Regular maintenance of filters, coils, and refrigerant charge.

**Lighting ECOs:**
- LED retrofits → 50 – 75 % reduction.
- Daylight harvesting via photosensors.
- Occupancy sensors in conference rooms, restrooms.
- Task lighting zoning.
- Maintain lux as per **IS 3646** (avoid over-illumination).

**BEE Star Labelling:**
- Replace 1-star appliances with **5-star BEE-rated** products.
- 5-star AC saves **35 %**; 5-star refrigerator saves **40 %**; 5-star fan saves **50 %**.
- Promotes long-term energy savings even after the audit period ends.

#### (b) Chiller ECM Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:** Capacity = 250 TR, $COP_{old} = 2.8$, $COP_{new} = 4.5$, Operating = 3000 h/yr, Tariff = ₹ 8/kWh, Capex = ₹ 35,00,000.

**Conversion Note:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW of refrigeration.

**Step 1 — Cooling load in kW:**

$$Q_{cooling} = 250 \times 3.517 = 879.25 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Old chiller power input:**

$$W_{old} = Q_{cooling} / COP_{old} = 879.25 / 2.8 = 314.02 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — New chiller power input:**

$$W_{new} = 879.25 / 4.5 = 195.39 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Power saved:**

$$\Delta W = 314.02 - 195.39 = 118.63 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy saved:**

$$\Delta E = \Delta W \times \text{operating hours} = 118.63 \times 3000 = 3{,}55{,}890 \, \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 6 — Annual monetary saving:**

$$A = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 3{,}55{,}890 \times 8 = ₹ \, 28{,}47{,}120 \approx ₹ \, 28.47 \, \text{lakh/year}$$

**Step 7 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \frac{\text{Capex}}{\text{Annual saving}} = \frac{35{,}00{,}000}{28{,}47{,}120} = 1.229 \, \text{years}$$

$$SPP \approx 14.7 \, \text{months}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Annual energy saved** = **3,55,890 kWh/year**
- **Annual monetary saving** = **₹ 28.47 lakh/year**
- **SPP** = **1.23 years (~14.7 months)** — strongly recommended ECM. ✅

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ECO with ECM:** Marks are lost when students use the terms interchangeably. Always say: *"An ECO was identified, and the corresponding ECM was implemented."*
>
> 2. **Wrong GCV units:** Furnace oil GCV is **10,200 kcal/kg = 42.7 MJ/kg**. Coal GCV varies (**3,500 – 5,500 kcal/kg**). Using the wrong constant loses 1 – 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Affinity Law cube:** Many students write $P \propto N^2$ (which is for *centrifugal pumps impeller diameter variation*, not speed). The correct statement is **Power $\propto$ Speed³** for speed variation.
>
> 4. **Neglecting units in Payback:** Always write the SPP result in **years or months**. Writing "SPP = 0.22" without unit is considered incomplete.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract PF penalty:** When computing savings from PF correction, include both the **penalty waiver** and the **I²R loss reduction** in the annual saving.
>
> 6. **Building U-value mistake:** Many students sum thermal conductivities instead of thermal resistances. Remember: $R = L/k$, and **resistances add, conductances do not**.
>
> 7. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state, *"Assuming pump efficiency remains constant at X %"* before applying affinity laws.
>
> 8. **M&V omission:** In ECM questions, if the question does not specify verification, **mention IPMVP Option A or B** to demonstrate professional awareness — this often earns a bonus mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ✅ **ECO** = Identified opportunity. **ECM** = Implemented measure.
- ✅ **Electrical ECOs** focus on motors (VSDs, IE3/IE4), transformers, PFC, lighting, cables, compressed air.
- ✅ **Affinity Law** for centrifugal loads: $P \propto N^3$ — **the most tested concept**.
- ✅ **PFC capacitor**: $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ kVAr.
- ✅ **Boiler efficiency**: $\eta = D(h_g - h_{fw}) / (GCV \cdot F)$.
- ✅ **Stack loss** is the largest boiler loss; reduce via **economizer + air preheater**.
- ✅ **Pinch Analysis** is the thermodynamic ECO tool for HEN optimization.
- ✅ **Distillation** is the single largest energy consumer in chemical plants (~ 40 %).
- ✅ **Waste Heat Recovery** options: Economizer, APH, WHB, ORC, Kalina cycle.
- ✅ **HVAC = ~ 50 %** of building energy; chiller COP improvement has the highest impact.
- ✅ **Setpoint discipline**: 24 °C cooling / 26 °C heating → ~ 6 % saving per °C.
- ✅ **BEE 5-star appliances** save 35 – 50 % over 1-star.
- ✅ **Rooftop solar PV** + **solar water heaters** are the highest-impact residential ECMs.
- ✅ **BMS / BEMS** deliver 10 – 25 % building-wide savings.
- ✅ **Financial Metrics**: SPP, NPV, IRR, DPP, LCC — know the formula for each.
- ✅ **Rule of Thumb SPP cutoffs**: < 2 yr = auto-approve; 2 – 4 yr = case-by-case; > 4 yr = escalate.
- ✅ **IPMVP** (Option A / B / C / D) is the verification standard after implementation.
- ✅ **India-specific anchors to memorize**: ECBC, ENS, BEE, PAT scheme, ESCO, GRIHA, IGBC, LEED-India, IS 3646, IS 3103 (industrial fans), IS 2064 (boilers).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "M-A-S-T-E-R" for Module 4:**
> - **M** = Motors (largest electrical load)
> - **A** = Affinity laws (Power ∝ Speed³)
> - **S** = Steam system (boiler + condensate + traps)
> - **T** = Thermodynamic pinch (HEN design)
> - **E** = Envelope + HVAC in buildings
> - **R** = Return on investment (payback / NPV)
